Using the "Skip_mash" parameter may be a way to get around this issue. Using that parameter makes the program take longer to run, but it will be just as accurate as if Mash was run, and in some cases even more accurate.

I'm worried that the underlying "permissions" issue may prevent other programs (like mummer, the program used is secondary clustering) from working as well. But if for some reason it's a mash-specific issue, using the "Skip_mash" parameter is a great idea.

Best,
-Matt

Original comment by: [unconscious](http://bitbucket.org/unconscious)

Confirmed on python 2.7 with identical traceback.

Actually, the library is ok, it's that ssl-cat.py doesn't call irc.connection.Factory the right way.

scripts/ssl-cat.py line 56 calls

```
#!python

    ssl_factory = irc.connection.Factory(ssl.wrap_socket)
```
when it should be

```
#!python

    ssl_factory = irc.connection.Factory(wrapper=ssl.wrap_socket)
```
resulting in the wrapper being assigned to the bind_address

-------------------------------------------------------------

Made a pull request with the fix
https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/pull-request/22/fix-factory-call-in-ssl-example-script/diff

Too bad it's only a one-liner :)

So, here's what i've found so far (by digging into pyswagger lol)

Looks like it's a pyswagger issue (i'm going to open a ticket there), as by default in swagger, arrays have a "collectionFormat" set to csv, but here, for an unknown reason it's always to "None"

Currently CCP (in ESI) do not set that value (as the default 'csv' is what they need), but something in pyswagger bugs, so when it tries to get the format (with a `getattr(obj, 'collectionFormat', 'csv')`), it always return "None" instead of 'csv'. That's why we have that issue.

So far, I've found a way to bypass this issue, **but it may break other things** (as I didn't test it with anything else). You'll just have to add these two line before creating the App:
```python
from pyswagger.spec.v2_0.objects import Parameter
Parameter.__swagger_fields__['collectionFormat']='csv'
```

And the result for your endpoint :
```python
>>> from pyswagger.spec.v2_0.objects import Parameter
>>> Parameter.__swagger_fields__['collectionFormat']='csv'

>>> from esipy import App, EsiClient

>>> app = App.create('https://esi.tech.ccp.is/latest/swagger.json')
>>> client = EsiClient()

>>> res=client.request(app.op['get_characters_names'](character_ids=[90000001]))
>>> res.data
[{u'character_id': 90000001, u'character_name': u'Vert1'}]
```

I'll keep you informed if I have other infos about this.

Per above, making sure there is something to select side-steps the problem.

Rather than just adding a dummy socket, the initial task could be something useful, like a monitor.

I see from this thread that the monitor is separate for a good reason:

https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/137

But curio could have an internal monitor too and that could be the first thing that is set up.

All things equal, use of multiprocessing with curio scares me a bit.  Curio has a fairly complex runtime environment potentially involving an async event loop, threads, and signal handling.   Internally, Curio uses multiprocessing.Process objects, but they are launched using the "spawn" method of multiprocessing which creates them in an entirely clean interpreter process. 

My general thought on external processes is to rely on the Channel machinery described here:  http://curio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#module-curio.channel

It's not stated in the docs, but I'm pretty sure Curio Channel objects are wire-compatible with multiprocessing connection objects.  So, one could have Curio on one side of the connection and normal synchronous Python code on the other (using multiprocessing.connection).

As a note: I'd never want to see direct usage of sleep(0) as the "solution" to this.   If I were solving this for real, it would disappear into the underlying socket class.  Or maybe a subclass of socket.
Now I know why I left this open... still thinking about the case of two tasks reading on the same file descriptor.   Behavior is currently undefined.  Maybe it should raise an exception.

Here is an unkillable task! Don't put the blame on
poor allow_cancellation. Any sufficiently determined
can eat up the cancellation. Probably there is nothing
can be done to protect againts it. So don't worry be happy.

And it may be better to review task management code
against the assumption of all well behaving tasks..
I guess best can be done is to assume cancellation
as just an advisory thing. No guarantees.. Sorry.

```python
from curio import spawn, sleep, run

async def cancel_if_you_can():
    while True:
        try:
            print('Alive!')
            await sleep(1)
        except:
            pass

async def main():
    inmortal = await spawn(cancel_if_you_can())
    while True:
        sleep(1)
        await inmortal.cancel()

run(main())
```

That would be a good thing; it just hasn't happened, although if you look at a few of the other issues, you will see that some users have done (and shared) some link code specific to their own needs.

pdfrw excels in print flows (where it's really fast) and in reusing parts of old PDFs with reportlab (where there's no free competition that I know of; reportlab's pagecatcher is quite expensive, I'm told).  Neither of those use-cases require link preservation, and I think other tools such as pyPdf2 and pdftk support links, so there hasn't really been that much pressure.  

PDFs are overcomplicated and nobody's submitted a PR with code (and tests!) to correctly preserve valid links for pdfrw yet (and toss the invalid ones).  Although many PDF viewers are really good at coping with incorrect PDFs, it is the goal of pdfrw not to produce those.  We also don't want dangling references that cause all the pages you thought you got rid of to wind up in the actual file, even though they're not viewable.

Yeah, negated shorthands `\D`, `\W` and `\S` are not recognised inside a charclass. This would have been fiddly but maybe I'll implement it sometime. Fortunately there is a trivial workaround, instead of writing `[\S]` just write `[^\s]` or `\S`, and instead of writing `[\S\s]` just write `.`. If you have something more complex like `[\Wdef]`, render it as `\W|[def]` and then do what `greenery` is designed to do:

```
>>> from greenery import lego
>>> str(lego.parse('\\W|[def]').reduce())
'[^0-9A-Z_abcg-z]'
```

How this should be set up is distribution specific. rc.local is certainly not the way to go since it's executed potentially before network access.

I definitely don't want to run this on system start *by default* because GeoIP is not perfect. For example, I work at Facebook, and our IP ranges often show America/Los_Angeles just because our AS is registered as being there, even though I'm in London. It would be very annoying to have this happen repeatedly.

I'd suggest that if you really want this, you either:

1. Run tzupdate after the network comes up (with systemd, by specifying `network.target` as a dependency
2. Run tzupdate on a cron every hour or so

I'm not going to have it set up by default on installation though, since this is very intrusive. It should be up to the user what they want to do with the tool, it shouldn't make assumptions about how they want to use it.

> The interface name may be '', in which case, the implementation will use the first interface it finds that supports a property with the given name.

...I'd argue, for the sake of consistency, that 3. above is effectively the case -- that txdbus will not be stricter than that -- and that, thus, fixing this involves fixing the test to accomodate for the possible outcomes.

* Could it be that the first system had a config file that specified a particular backend? Make sure that backend includes the full path to the desired backend. Try without the config file.
* Confirm that the module actually exists. Find where keyring is installed and look for 'keyring/backends/file.py'.

I was going to suggest other things to try like importing 'keyring.backends.file' manually, but I realize now there's no way to get at the backends without causing everything to be imported (as a side-effect of `import keyring`).

It's conceivable there's a race condition when importing a certain backend before another. However, I wouldn't expect that to happen using the default configuration (as the import behavior is very similar for all platforms) - so I'd still suspect the behavior is triggered by something in configuration.

Please let me know what you find.

I'm assuming this is fixed. I can't wait for the machines to catch up to verify the fix. If you don't find the LICENCE file in the 12.0.1 release, ping me here.

Jason,

Thanks for the response.  I'm going to share two assumptions I have which is driving my perspective on this issue:

1. Anyone going through the extra work of using the keyring library wants a more secure implementation than plaintext, otherwise they would likely have just used a config file.
2. Many/most developers don't read documentation and will use default settings unless the program flat-out doesn't work, at which point they will read more closely.

I realize that these assumptions may not always be valid, but IMO we should approach the solution to this issue assuming the above is true and then allowing devs to make exceptions as needed.

> Although the use of the priority < 1 seems arbitrary, the measure has a documented purpose. A priority of < 1, > 0 is suitable but a priority >= 1 is recommended. I believe that's the best indicator keyring can give about backends without more detail about the user's environment and usage.

I understand that there is some ambiguity here based on a user's use-case.  I guess, I would argue, that the plain-text option is really "unsuitable" in most instances and sometimes suitable.  Maybe the terms should be updated to "available" and "recommended."

> One would be to move the Plaintext keyring out of the project altogether and require it to be included explicitly as a separate package if the user desires for it to be available.

Seems like too much work to me.  :)

> A less invasive approach could be to disable the Plaintext Keyring by default, and only enable it by API or environment variable (opt-in)

I like this.  Although, I'd like to adjust the proposal to make anything less than recommended opt-in.  That way, the default implementation is most secure and there is extra effort required on the devs part to make less secure.  Requiring extra effort will also likely lead to the dev reading the docs more closely and understanding better the tradeoffs for what they are choosing to do.

I propose a api variable something like:

```
#!python

keyring.allow_unrecommended_backends = True

```

or, if you don't like the connotation of "unrecommended", then maybe:

```
#!python

keyring.allow_less_secure_backends = True

```

Either one will, IMO, encourage devs to read the docs more carefully and understand the tradeoffs of which backend they are using.

If no recommended backends are available, and the API setting isn't enabled, then I would prefer to see an exception thrown indicating such.

I realize this would lead to some backwards incompatibility issues.  IMO, that is acceptable.  In order to ease the transition, I would encourage you to make a an initial release that issues a deprecation warning if an unrecommended backend is used by default without the explicit opt-in.  Then, make a new major release 3-6 months later which implements the API change.

> Another approach could involve raising a UserWarning when passwords are saved in the plaintext keyring (and implementers could suppress that warning in the API or with an environment variable).

This is better than nothing, but I like the above option better.  If you are going to require the dev to make changes anyway, then lets just make the implementation opt-in.  Most secure by default, less secure if they choose.

> The latter two options might prove inconvenient on systems where security of the file is not a concern.

Based on my first assumption, this is going to be the less-common case.  Therefore, I'm proposing that we accept the inconvenience in favor of a default implementation that is most secure.

> I'm eager to hear your responses on these proposals. I still agree that it's important to address this issue and find a less surprising outcome in this case.

Thanks for inviting feedback.  I hope my thoughts are helpful.

No, I don't think your tip will work. Look at `_write_elements()`, `fcd` is an empty array, meshio currently does not dump the the physical and elementary tags. I expect many software out there to expect these tags to apply material coefficients or boundary conditions. This is certainly the case of PETSc-based codes. In other words, and IMHO, the right way to specify regions in Gmsh files is in the `$Element` section, the other `$XXXData` sections serve a different purpose (e.g, attach solution data for postprocessing with the Gmsh GUI).

Please try the following: Create a msh file natively with Gmsh. Next read and write back to a new file using meshio. Finally, open the new file with Gmsh GUI. You should notice that all region information is lost, and the Gmsh GUI is basically interpreting the `$ElementData` as a scalar solution field on cells, and all labels are lost (you can turn on labels visibility with Options -> Mesh (in the left pane) -> Visibility (top tab) and clicking on the "Surface labels" checkbox)

You should be able to get negative weights in WeighMeanVar

Try setting `bounds=(None, None)` in the constuctor for WeighMeanVar. The scipy minimization routine should then set no bounds on the weights. It may not always work though.


There is a closed form solution for the mean variance optimal portfolio with only a fully invested constraint and no long only constraint. It is probably better to just use that in this case. It's equation 1.32 in [here](https://faculty.washington.edu/ezivot/econ424/portfolioTheoryMatrix.pdf). Or simply `V^{-1} u / (1^{t} V^{-1} u)` where V is the covariance matrix and u is the expected excess return.

I don't know about other packages, but what I do for ruamel.yaml is try and compile a small program (which also tests if the library libyaml is installed), and if that doesn't work ext_modules in setup.py is set to None.

It don't try to do this test-compile if '--version' is in sys.argv, or '--restructuredtext' or 'sdist' as those indicate invocations by pip that are done during my build process that don't involve actual installation (you probably only have to compile when pip calls setup.py with `bdist` in sys.argv).
The actual compilation is done by initialising and using the distutils/setuptools compiler, so I'm using what will be used later on for the actual compile. 

Testing what happens when the compiler is not there can easily be faked by temporarily setting the environment variables `CC` and `CXX` to non point to non-existing scripts.

I along with my colleague were trying to debug this issue as it causes our own test suite to fail causing problems. It seems like this issue arises from `urllib.parse.parse_qsl` in the standard library specifically [here][1]. The method looks something like this:

```
def parse_qsl(qs, keep_blank_values=False, strict_parsing=False,
              encoding='utf-8', errors='replace'):
            qs, _coerce_result = _coerce_args(qs)
            .....
            value = _coerce_result(value)
            r.append((name, value))
    return r

# For 3.2, we deliberately require applications that
# handle improperly quoted URLs to do their own
# decoding and encoding. If valid use cases are
# presented, we may relax this by using latin-1
# decoding internally for 3.3
_implicit_encoding = 'ascii'
_implicit_errors = 'strict'
```
Note the `_implicit_encoding` or `ascii` above which is what `_coerce_result` method uses to try to force the result to instead of passing on the `encoding` variable which _correctly_ defaults to `utf-8`.


[1]: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/urllib/parse.py#L703

The "wierd" behavior for `user.deviantart.com` is just a leftover from when the deviantart module only had 1 extractor and didn't even use the API. Back then one could only get all images of a user's gallery and providing this URL as "shortcut" seemed appropriate.


> Well, at first sight, not relying on gallery/all seems to make sense, as long as the two other endpoints work in the same manner.

I tried this for a couple of users and just using the contents of their gallery-folders didn't work all the time. Without the "Featured" folder you can get less then their actual Deviation count; with the "Featured" folder you get more than that (duplicates, probably).
For a user with 300 Deviations I got 200 without and 400 with. So you either get only a subset or duplicates.


> A gallery folder that also contains folders

I've found an example of that: http://rachychan.deviantart.com/gallery/46449774/Pokemon
This sub-folder has another folder in it. Seems that an account needs Core Membership to be able to create folders inside of folders.
It it technically possible to preserve the given folder structure: each folder object in an API response has a "parentid" member that refers to its parent folder, so one could reconstruct the whole directory tree. It's just that there is no good way of implementing variable-depth directory paths with gallery-dl's infrastructure.


> Another thing from the API documentation: GET /user/profile/{username}

Doesn't work for groups:
```
Request
GET https://www.deviantart.com/api/v1/oauth2/user/profile/cgpinups

Result
{
    "error": "invalid_request",
    "error_description": "user not found.",
    "error_code": 2,
    "status": "error"
}

```

There are actually undocumented API endpoints for groups that don't really work: https://github.com/DeviantArt/DeviantArt-API/issues/122


> We should first agree on reasonable defaults

That, and consistent behavior, are quite important, I think. For that reason I tried to compile a list of all possible URLs for a Deviantart user:

| #| URL | Status | |
| ---:| --- |:---:| --- |
| 1| user.deviantart.com/                          |✔| Currently the same as (2). Should probably do all of (2), (7) and (10)
| 2| user.deviantart.com/gallery/                  |✔| All deviations/images in [user]'s gallery
| 3| user.deviantart.com/gallery/?catpath=/        |✔| Same as (2).
| 4| user.deviantart.com/gallery/?catpath=/some/category/path |✖| No way of filtering these per API, might be possible to do the filtering manually
| 5| user.deviantart.com/gallery/?catpath=scraps   |✖| Not possible to get these per API, as far as I can tell
| 6| user.deviantart.com/gallery/123/folder-name   |✔| All deviations in this gallery folder
| 7| user.deviantart.com/favourites/               |✔| The contents of the "Featured" folder of [user]'s favorites. Inconsistent with (2) and (10), all of these should do the same.
| 8| user.deviantart.com/favourites/?catpath=/     |✔| All favorites of [user]. This is done by retrieving the contents of all favorite folders of that user. There is no actual API endpoint for this.
| 9| user.deviantart.com/favourites/123/folder-name|✔| All favorites in that specific folder/collection
|10| user.deviantart.com/journal/                  |✔| All of [user]'s journals
|11| user.deviantart.com/blog/                     |✔| Same as (10) (Groups use `/blog`, Users use `/journal`)
|12| user.deviantart.com/journal/?catpath=/        |✔| Same as (10)
|13| user.deviantart.com/journal/name-123          |✔| That specific journal entry. Should go in the same directory as (12)
|14| user.deviantart.com/art/name-123              |✔| That specific deviation. Should go in the same directory as the items of (3)

My suggestion would be that (2), (7) and (10) provide the contents of all the available (sub)folders (just "Featured" for journals) and put them in appropriately named directories:
```
deviantart/
    gallery/
        Featured/
            image1.jpg
            ...
        Folder 1/
            image123.jpg
            ...
    favourites/
        Featured/
        Collection 1/
    journal/
```

(3), (8) and (12) would just produce a flat list:
```
deviantart/
    gallery/
        image1.jpg
        ...
    favourites/
        image123.jpg
        ...
```

Not quite sure what to do, but the way things currently work is not consistent and defies expectations quite a bit.
Another example of this are usernames: sometimes the value of `author[username]` gets used, which is included in the actual API response and may contain capital letters, and sometimes the `collection[owner]` value get used, which is just the lowercase username taken from the URL.

For me this is too general to be included in TinyDB since it has very little to do with the underlying database, i.e. querying, IO operations, etc. It is easy to write a key function that one could pass to `sorted` to order data by specific keys.  - We can do querying better since:
    - we know the underlying file format
    - we have a query cache

Of course exceptions could be made where necessary for pragmatism, but I hope you see my point.

All EtherNet/IP CIP I/O in the Cpppo library use "Unconnected" sends.  The key to faster I/O is to set up the connection, and then to use it without closing it.  The client.main API always sets up, uses, and then closes a connection.  By maintaining your own connection and then using it repeatedly, you get much higher transaction rates.

To improve this much, much further -- use the client.pipeline API.  This allows you to issue multiple transaction requests, before receiving the transaction replies.  You can keep the PLC "saturated" with requests, instead of letting it sit idle while you await the response to a previous request.

Hi @jrosebr1,  you bet.
The expected input is a python list of images, where the list can be of any length and the images of any width/height.
The user specifies the how many "tiles" in the x and y direction as well as the height/width of each "tile".
The output will then be a single image containing all of the input images laid out in a grid.
If the amount of images in the python list is greater than the number of tiles in the grid (which is usually the case), then the list simply spills into a new grid image of same dimensions. And since this is usually the case, the output is always a python list of the output grid images, even if a single image is output. Any empty space left over on a grid image is filled with black pixels.

The following is the code to be added to `convenience.py` , followed by an example usage:
```
def make_grids_of_images(image_list, image_shape, grid_shape):
    """
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    author: Kyle Hounslow
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    Converts a list of single images into a list of 'grid' images of specified rows and columns.
    A new grid image is started once rows and columns of grid image is filled.
    Empty space of incomplete grid images are filled with black pixels
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    :param image_list: python list of input images
    :param image_shape: tuple, size each image will be resized to for display (width, height)
    :param grid_shape: tuple, shape of image grid (width, height)
    :return: list of grid images in numpy array format
    ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    example usage:

    # load single image
    img = cv2.imread('lena.jpg')
    # duplicate image 25 times
    num_imgs = 25
    img_list = []
    for i in xrange(num_imgs):
        img_list.append(img)
    # convert image list into a grid of 256x256 images tiled in a 5x5 grid
    grids = make_grids_of_images(img_list, (256, 256), (5, 5))
    # iterate through grids and display
    for grid in grids:
        cv2.imshow('grid image', grid)
        cv2.waitKey(0)

    ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    """
    if len(image_shape) != 2:
        raise Exception('image shape must be list or tuple of length 2 (rows, cols)')
    if len(grid_shape) != 2:
        raise Exception('grid shape must be list or tuple of length 2 (rows, cols)')
    image_grids = []
    # start with black canvas to draw images onto
    grid_image = np.zeros(shape=(image_shape[1] * (grid_shape[1]), image_shape[0] * grid_shape[0], 3),
                          dtype=np.uint8)
    cursor_pos = [0, 0]
    start_new_img = False
    for img in image_list:
        if type(img).__module__ != np.__name__:
            raise Exception('input of type {} is not a valid numpy array'.format(type(img)))
        start_new_img = False
        img = cv2.resize(img, image_shape)
        # draw image to black canvas
        grid_image[cursor_pos[1]:cursor_pos[1] + image_shape[1], cursor_pos[0]:cursor_pos[0] + image_shape[0]] = img
        cursor_pos[0] += image_shape[0]  # increment cursor x position
        if cursor_pos[0] >= grid_shape[0] * image_shape[0]:
            cursor_pos[1] += image_shape[1]  # increment cursor y position
            cursor_pos[0] = 0
            if cursor_pos[1] >= grid_shape[1] * image_shape[1]:
                cursor_pos = [0, 0]
                image_grids.append(grid_image)
                # reset black canvas
                grid_image = np.zeros(shape=(image_shape[1] * (grid_shape[1]), image_shape[0] * grid_shape[0], 3),
                                      dtype=np.uint8)
                start_new_img = True
    if start_new_img is False:
        image_grids.append(grid_image)  # add unfinished grid
    return image_grids
```

> Presumably, a variant of `nbstripout` could also be used to add a git filter that would automatically run a notebook when commiting it to a repository to ensure that all its output cells *are* populated?

Not easily: as mentioned, npstripout uses a [git clean/smudge filter](https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Customizing-Git-Git-Attributes) and operates purely on the file level. No cells are ever executed.

You would need to look at a pre-commit hook, however I expect that's not too easy to set up: you'd need to start a notebook server, run the notebook and deal with failures. This would also take very long.

If you only want to *verify* the output is populated, that's easier to do (and you could potentially reuse some of `nbstripout`'s code for that).

```python
def download(req, **user_kw):
    pool = _create_onetime_pool()

```

Second, in `telepot/__init__.py`, modify a section within the method `download_file`:
```python
            r = api.download((self._token, f['file_path']), preload_content=False)

            while 1:
                data = r.read(self._file_chunk_size)
                if not data:
                    break
                d.write(data)
```
Note **two** changes: the `preload_content=False` argument, and `if not data` above the `break`.

You need `preload_content=False` to properly `read()` later on. And `data` is an empty string (not `None`) after stream ends, so we have to check it using `if not data`.

This isn't a global function. It's a function of a `Client`. You need a `discord.Client` instance and you need to run this function in one of the events of the library. e.g.

```py
# have a client instance:
client = discord.Client()

# somewhere inside an async def:
await client.change_nickname(some_member_obj, new_nickname_string)
```

See the [documentation](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.Client.change_nickname).

If you have the channel's ID, you can use [`get_channel`](https://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/rewrite/api.html#discord.Client.get_channel).

For future questions like this, you should join either the [official discord.py server](https://discord.gg/r3sSKJJ) or the [Discord API server](https://discord.gg/discord-api) for help, as the README recommends.

writing is most safe when you first write your entire new file to the same filesystem as the final file should reside on. Then you can verify the write was successful, and use a move operation to rename the file to the actual filename you wanted. Yes, including node_modules inside the static directory isn't going to end well so I'd avoid that if at all possible. The setup I generally have is:
```
./static_src (which possibly includes node_modules)
        ↓
  [frontend build tool de jour]
        ↓
./static_build
        ↓
  [ manage.py collectstatic ]
        ↓
./static_root
```
So Django doesn't need to know anything about the build tool I'm using or how the original source files are laid out, it just picks up the build artifacts from `static_build`.

There are definitely some optimisations that can be made (using `scandir` and some other tricks) to reduce the amount of disk IO during start up and I'll look into doing that before too long.

@oppianmatt The static manifest isn't sufficient by itself, because we need to know the sizes of all the files and also whether the corresponding compressed versions exist.

It ought to be possible to cache the entirety of WhiteNoise's state by pickling the `self.files` attribute to a file somewhere, but then that's an extra layer of complexity to manage which probably won't be worthwhile for most users.

The file scan is happening for each worker because gunicorn is configured to boot multiple versions of the app, each in its own process. You can use the [preload](http://docs.gunicorn.org/en/stable/settings.html#server-mechanics) option to boot a single copy of the app and then fork it into multiple workers.

### Short Explanation:
Python 3 uses unicode by default while python 2 differentiates between ASCII and unicode.

Therefore, if `text.encode('utf-8')` is applied on an (encoded) unicode string in python 2 it will result in
`UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte ... in position 1: ordinal not in range(128)`.

### Longer Explanation:

In python 3, `type(u'Köln')` and `type('Köln')` both give `<class 'str'>` and class `'str'` is encoded to class `'bytes'`.
In python 2,  type(u'Köln') gives <type 'unicode'>, type('Köln') gives <type 'str'>, and type `'unicode'` is encoded to type` 'str'`.

Since function `annotate()` requires input of type 'str' any unicode string in python 2 has to be passed as encoded `'str'` and further calls of `text.encode('utf-8')` will result in `UnicodeDecodeError`.

Consequently, `text.encode('utf-8')` should only be called in python 3.

@Amorik the conversion step you did, and the subsequent configuration based on those files is correct. I'll add this to the NiPyAPI documentation (it might also be helpful in the NiFi tls-toolkit documentation). If you are interested in extracting the cert/key from the JKS stores, here are the steps I use for that, which were used to generate the keys for the [secure_connection.py](https://github.com/Chaffelson/nipyapi/blob/master/nipyapi/demo/secure_connection.py) demo:

    # use NiFi tls-toolkit to generate CA, server key/cert, client key/cert
    ./nifi-toolkit-1.4.0/bin/tls-toolkit.sh standalone --certificateAuthorityHostname localhost  --hostnames localhost --subjectAlternativeNames nifi,registry,secure-nifi,secure-registry --nifiDnSuffix ", OU=nifi" --keyStorePassword localhostKeystorePassword --trustStorePassword localhostTruststorePassword --clientCertDn "CN=user1, OU=nifi" --clientCertPassword clientPassword --days 9999 --outputDirectory nipyapi-tests
     
    # change to tls-toolkit output directory
    cd ./nipyapi-tests
     
    # copy server's key/trust stores
    mkdir keys
    cp localhost/keystore.jks keys/localhost-ks.jks
    cp localhost/truststore.jks keys/localhost-ts.jks
     
    # create a Java Key Store (JKS) from the client key
    keytool -importkeystore \
      -srckeystore CN=user1_OU=nifi.p12 -srcstoretype PKCS12 -srcstorepass clientPassword \
      -destkeystore keys/client-ks.jks -deststoretype JKS -deststorepass clientPassword -destkeypass clientPassword 
     
    # copy keys and certificates from JKS format into PKCS12 format:
    cd ./keys
    keytool -importkeystore \
      -srckeystore client-ks.jks -srcstoretype jks -srcstorepass clientPassword \
      -destkeystore client-ks.p12 -deststoretype pkcs12 -deststorepass clientPassword
    keytool -importkeystore \
      -srckeystore localhost-ts.jks -srcstoretype jks -srcstorepass localhostTruststorePassword \
      -destkeystore localhost-ts.p12 -deststoretype pkcs12 -deststorepass localhostTruststorePassword
      
    # copy the CA certificate from PKCS12 format to PEM format:
    openssl pkcs12 -in localhost-ts.p12 -passin pass:localhostTruststorePassword -out localhost-ts.pem -nokeys
    openssl pkcs12 -in client-ks.p12 -passin pass:clientPassword -out client-cert.pem -nokeys
    openssl pkcs12 -in client-ks.p12 -passin pass:clientPassword -out client-key.pem -passout pass:clientPassword

You should now have a directory with the following contents:

    keys/
      +-- client-cert.pem   # client public cert (PEM format, unencrypted)
      +-- client-key.pem    # client private key (PEM format, encrypted):  password=clientPassword
      +-- client-ks.jks     # client key/cert keystore (JKS format):     keystorePass=clientPassword, keyPass=clientPassword
      +-- client-ks.pk12    # client key/cert keystore (PKCS12 format):  keystorePass=clientPassword, keyPass=clientPassword
      +-- localhost-ks.jks  # server key/cert keystore (JKS format):     keystorePass=localhostKeystorePassword, keyPass=localhostKeystorePassword
      +-- localhost-ts.jks  # server/client CA cert truststore (JKS format):     truststorePass=localhostTruststorePassword
      +-- localhost-ts.p12  # server/client CA cert truststore (PKCS12 format):  truststorePass=localhostTruststorePassword
      +-- localhost-ts.pem  # server/client CA cert truststore (PEM format):     truststorePass=localhostTruststorePassword
     
You can verify the contents of these keystores using the following commands:

    keytool -list -v -keystore keys/client-ks.jks -storepass clientPassword
    keytool -list -v -keystore keys/localhost-ks.jks -storepass localhostKeystorePassword
    keytool -list -v -keystore keys/localhost-ts.jks -storepass localhostTruststorePassword

This guide is also available at: https://gist.github.com/kevdoran/70447c312ca955c3a39b15cbfafb5123

If you have any other questions regarding SSL/TLS configuration with NiFi or NiPyAPI, let me know!

Aha. I've got the app in broken state, with chrome debugger on it and fixed the bug. How amazing is chrome debugger. Messages were getting to the app, but causing a load of exceptions.

The problem was on line 33 of connection.js, where it calls 'string.startsWith'. 

Turns out `string.startsWith` is only supported by very recent versions of the chrome webview. On my phone, whilst I had latest chrome, I didn't have 'android webview' installed from play store, so it was just using the one that came with my phone originally. The fix was to install "android webview" from play store, so it updates to the latest version.

I think you could be on the safe side and add a startswith definition to connection.js anyway, like the below, and I think this would fix it without needing the webview update:
 
    if ( typeof String.prototype.startsWith != 'function' ) {
      String.prototype.startsWith = function( str ) {
        return this.substring( 0, str.length ) === str;
      }
    };

First, think of `Sequence` like a `struct` that has defined fields, some of which might be optional.  Then think of `SequenceOf` like a Python list.  In Python terms, the weekly schedule is a list of daily schedules, one for each day.  The `DailySchedule` object has one attribute, a `daySchedule`, which is a list of `TimeValue` objects.  The `time` attribute is going to be the time of day that the schedule value changes from whatever it was to the `value` attribute.

For example, this daily schedule says that on some day the schedule will have its default value until 8:00am when it changes to 8, then goes back to its default value until 5:00pm when it changes to 42, then goes back to default at 7:00pm:

    >>> from bacpypes.primitivedata import Integer, Null
    >>> from bacpypes.basetypes import DailySchedule, TimeValue
    >>> 
    >>> x = DailySchedule(
    ...     daySchedule=[
    ...     TimeValue(time=(8,0,0,0), value=Integer(8)),
    ...     TimeValue(time=(14,0,0,0), value=Null()),
    ...     TimeValue(time=(17,0,0,0), value=Integer(42)),
    ...     TimeValue(time=(19,0,0,0), value=Null()),
    ...     ])

These objects have a `debug_contents()` method, so you can call it on each of the seven daily schedules you have been given:

    >>> x.debug_contents()
        daySchedule
            [0]            time = (8, 0, 0, 0)
                value = <bacpypes.primitivedata.Integer object at 0x7f01ae7d32b0>
            [1]            time = (14, 0, 0, 0)
                value = <bacpypes.primitivedata.Null object at 0x7f01af9272b0>
            [2]            time = (17, 0, 0, 0)
                value = <bacpypes.primitivedata.Integer object at 0x7f01af921898>
            [3]            time = (19, 0, 0, 0)
                value = <bacpypes.primitivedata.Null object at 0x7f01ae6cbe80>
    >>> 

And if that seems a little too obscure, it also has a `dict_contents()` method:

    >>> x.dict_contents()
    {'daySchedule': [{'time': (8, 0, 0, 0), 'value': 8}, {'time': (14, 0, 0, 0), 'value': ()},
    {'time': (17, 0, 0, 0), 'value': 42}, {'time': (19, 0, 0, 0), 'value': ()}]}

> _Warning: The dictionary returned by `dict_contents()` is likely to change to JSON-LD sometime in the future._

In the interim, I checked out what the problem and it looks like it's Twitter being inconsistent in the rate limit returned in their URL headers, which may be a temporary problem or may be something that we need to take into account. For instance, for the code:

```python
for i in range(0, 175):
    api.GetSearch(term="primary", count=100)
    print(api.rate_limit.get_limit('/search/tweets'))
```

I am essentially getting two sets of rate limits:

```
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=134, reset=1465417711)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=25, reset=1465417814)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=133, reset=1465417711)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=24, reset=1465417814)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=23, reset=1465417814)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=22, reset=1465417814)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=21, reset=1465417814)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=132, reset=1465417711)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=20, reset=1465417814)
EndpointRateLimit(limit=180, remaining=19, reset=1465417814)
```

Where one (the lower one) is the real rate limit and the higher one is maybe cached on their servers or whatever server is counting their rate limits is being inconsistently load balanced? I'm not sure.

What I'd suggest you do in the meantime (since there's nothing we can do about this right now), is something like:

```python
for user in users:
    results = api.GetSearch(
        term="from:"+user,
        since_id=sincetweet,
        max_id=maxtweet,
        count=10,
        result_type="recent")
    time.sleep(5.05)
```

The time.sleep(5.05) call will ensure that you never go over the rate limit for the GetSearch endpoint (15 minute window (900 seconds) divided by 180 allowed calls + a small amount of padding).

In that case it might make sense to expand upon the current idea of using just:
```py
def get_value():
    if self.getter_callback:
        self._value = self.to_valid_value(value=self.getter_callback())
    return self._value
```
and incorporate the notify call if the new value is different. Something like:
```py
def get_value():
    if not self.getter_callback:
        raise ValueError('No getter_callback defined')
    value = self.to_valid_value(value=self.getter_callback())
    if value != self.value:
        self.value = value
        self.notify()
```

You may edit the `start` query parameter, and make multiple calls in batches of 10:

search_google cats --start=0

```python
import search_google.api

# Define buildargs for cse api
buildargs = {
  'serviceName': 'customsearch',
  'version': 'v1',
  'developerKey': 'your_api_key'
}

cseargs = {
  'q': 'keyword query',
  'cx': 'your_cse_id'
}

# Create a results object
results = []

The first parser was written from scratch and it is inside legacy_translators folder. It did not generate AST, it was generating Python code instantly. Since I did not know anything about parser before and I basically invented it on my own it suffered from poor design and had numerous bugs. To fix that I decided to use esprima.js and that was before esprima provided full support for JS 6. I translated esprima to python manually :) Of course you can just use Js2Py to translate new version of esprima.js to python and enjoy a JS 6 parser (it is present in examples folder) but that would affect performance. So the best option would be to simply modify the manual translation. Unfortunately, to support JS 6 we would have to implement all the extra built in objects and the process is rather boring and time consuming and this is the primary reason why I decided not to support JS 6.

Good start, but that cannot be it.

If `items()` returns a list, then the lambda will still keep the entire contents in memory, even if `iteritems()` returns it piecemeal. The large variable is just anonymously hidden in the closure, but it's still there.

If `iteritems()` is defined in terms of `items()`, then we need `items()` to be an iterator.

I'm not sure why it's like this now; I think it should be the other way around: `items()` defined in terms of `iteritems()`, namely `items = list(iteritems)`. That's because `iteritems` is the more general (and harder to implement) of the two.

Hello,

You can't run the scripts directly on .config files like that. The scripts expect the argument to be a Kconfig file.

.config files (along with e.g. \*_defconfig files) are particular generated configurations. For example, if you enable support for some particular filesystem and save the configuration, then this setting ends up in the .config file.

Kconfig files on the other hand list the available configuration options and their dependencies. When you run e.g. `make menuconfig`, it's the Kconfig files that let menuconfig know what options are available and how they're related.

The proper way to run the script is to apply the Makefile patch and then do

     $ make scriptconfig SCRIPT=Kconfiglib/examples/print_tree.py

You'll find this particular example in the documentation too, which also explains why it's necessary to through `make`.

Oh, and I just noticed that the Makefile patch doesn't apply with Linus' latest kernel due to a few lines were added to the beginning of scripts/kconfig/Makefile. It should be simple to apply manually though, as it's just a block of text that needs to be inserted near the beginning of scripts/kconfig/Makefile. I'll see if I can make it more robust somehow.

Getting closer, first working transfer sent.

I have two new secondary directives when designing the API:

1. State can be saved and restored. Goal: Make it usable from a web platform where objects don't persist between page views. The bank can require that a TAN is sent in the same dialogue as the original message, so we need to be able to resume dialogues.
2. Make the API so that future extensions don't break it. Specifically: Allow for the inclusion of other security mechanisms (e.g. HBCI, one-step TAN) that *don't* require two steps.

ad 1) Looks like this:
````python
client = FinTS3PinTanClient(..., set_data=None)
with client:
    response = client.start_sepa_transfer(...)
    dialog_data = client.pause_dialog()

challenge_data = response.get_data()
client_data = client.get_data()

# Store challenge_data, dialog_data and client_data out-of-band somewhere
# Ask the user to respond to response.hitan.challenge
# ... Some time passes ...
# Later, possibly in a different process, restore the state

client = FinTS3PinTanClient(..., set_data=client_data)
challenge = NeedRetryResponse.from_data(challenge_data)
with client.resume_dialog(dialog_data):
    client.send_tan(challenge, tan)
````

ad 2) Methods like `start_sepa_transfer()` return either the final response value (not implemented yet :), or an instance of (a subclass of) `NeedRetryResponse`. Kind of like a future, it will store both the current state and the next step to execute. In the PIN/TAN case: if you get an instance of `NeedTANResponse`, you need to ask the user for the TAN, then call `.send_tan()` which will complete the original `start_sepa_transfer()` call.


The developer point of view is:
````python
def start_sepa_transfer(...):
        return self._send_with_possible_retry(dialog, seg, self._continue_start_sepa_transfer)

def _continue_start_sepa_transfer(self, command_seg, response):
        # FIXME Properly find return code
        return True
````
where `_send_with_possible_retry(self, dialog, command_seg, resume_func)` will either directly call its `resume_func` argument (if no second step is necessary) or leave that up to `.send_tan()`.

As of now I've not done HKTAN#6 *on purpose* since that implies Strong Customer Authentication (SCA) which comes with its own set of cans of worms (including TANs for dialogue initiation/"login", TAN exemptions for certain commands based on bank decisions).

A few points after reflecting on the `attrs` API:

1. `attrs` requires a class, not an instance, as the argument to `attr.fields(cls)`. I'd prefer that `dataclass.fields` accepts either a class or instance. For comparison, `namedtuple` and `typing.NamedTuple` allow either for the similar `._fields` attribute (because it's a class attribute there).
```
>>> T = namedtuple('T', 'x y')
>>> T._fields
('x', 'y')
>>> T(0, 1)._fields
('x', 'y')
```

2. `attrs` does type checking, and will raise an `attrs`-specific exception if it's not an `attrs` class:
```
>>> attr.fields(object)
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
attr.exceptions.NotAnAttrsClassError: <class 'object'> is not an attrs-decorated class.
```
I'd rather just live with the `AttributeError`, since the attribute name points you in the right direction:
```
>>> dataclass.fields(object)
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
AttributeError: type object 'object' has no attribute '__dataclass_fields__'
```
Especially so because I'm planning on adding a helper function to detect if a class/instance was created with `@dataclass`, so you can LBLY if you want.

`frozen=True` prevents add new attributes after construction:

```python
my_item = InventoryItem(name='pizza', unit_price=6.99, quantity_on_hand=5)
my_item.absurb_headline = "Python attacks!"  # Can't add new attributes
```

`immutable=True` prevents attributes from being modified after construction:

```python
my_item = InventoryItem(name='pizza', unit_price=6.99, quantity_on_hand=5)
my_item.name = "Guido"  # AttributeError thrown. Guido is not for sale.
```

Both are important, but orthogonal. If I were using data classes, I'd often set both to `True`.

Ah it's an issue indeed, actually subcircuits are registered with a **name** set by `__name__` from SubCircuitFactory

```
class Netlist:
    def subcircuit(self, subcircuit):
        self._subcircuits[str(subcircuit.name)] = subcircuit

class SubCircuitFactory(SubCircuit):
    __name__ = None
    __nodes__ = None
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(self.__name__, *self.__nodes__, **kwargs)
```

Thus you should get the last one ( first one is overwritten ), which is an unexpected behaviour.

**We have to find the right way to provide a different name.**

Is

```
circuit.subcircuit(name, cls)
```

a better option ?

There are two ways to achieve this right now:

1. Use the [`RatelimitMiddleware`](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#middleware) and a custom `RATELIMIT_VIEW` that returns 429.
2. The [`Ratelimited`](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage.html#ratelimit.exceptions.Ratelimited) exception extends `PermissionDenied`, so a custom `handler403` could also return 429 for this exception type.

I don't [see any way](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.8.1/django/views/defaults.py#L67) to coerce the built-in 403 handler to send anything other than a 403.

I don't think we should ship a default error view because we can't know what content-type (json/xml/html) or shape of data to send back for a given API or endpoint. So right now we do it a little wrong, with little code. I suspect this would involve writing more code and imposing new requirements to be only very slightly less wrong.

Maybe it's worth explicitly calling out in the docs, though? "Want 429? Here's how..."

To calculate an ETA you will need a maximum value of course, but that doesn't have to be a fixed number. Depending on the progress bar you can just set a default maximum value and adjust it while using the progress bar to rescale it.

For example:
```
import time
import progressbar

bar = progressbar.ProgressBar()
bar.max_value = 100
for i in range(200):
    bar.update(i)
    bar.max_value = 100 + (i / 2)
    time.sleep(0.1)
```

Thanks for your comments! The loop in `post_ratelimited()` actually does have a safeguard; have a look at the `max_wait` and `max_redirects` values. If those two don't cover all possible paths in the code, please let me know.

What you're probably seeing is an artifact of the very large `max_wait` value. If a request fails, the loop will wait for increasing amounts of time, up to 1 hour, before bailing. The reason for this is that in my experience servers often malfunction for short periods of time, either because of migrations, maintenance tasks, misconfigurations or heavy load, or because the connecting user has hit a throttling policy limit.

If the loop exited early, consumers of exchangelib that don't implement their own rate-limiting code could quickly swamp such a server with new requests. That would only make things worse. Instead, it's better if the request loop waits patiently until the server is functioning again.

If the connecting user has hit a throttling policy, then the server will only respond with a HTTP 401, as if the password is wrong. The only cure is to stop making requests. There is no other indication AFAIK, so the code can't distinguish between the two situations.

Anyway, I'd be happy for any contributions to the project!

`.all()` is greedy and fetches all items in one go. Attachments load their content when accessing `attachment.content` or `attachment.item`, which will increase your memory usage while iterating.

If you have attributes that you don't care about, you can optimize your memory usage by fetching only the attributes you need:

```
for m in account.inbox.all().only('item_id', 'changekey', 'attachments'):
    ...
```

You could use multiple pictures of the same person a number of different ways. It just depends on your exact use case.

If you run `face_recognition.face_encodings(picture_of_me)[0]` on 30 pictures of the same person, you'll have 30 different encodings of the same person. Each encoding is an array of 128 numbers.

All 30 *should* be almost equal to each other (within 0.6 in euclidian distance across all 128 numbers), but of course that might not be the case. So you could handle that different ways:

1. You could compare each unknown picture separately against all 30 known samples of the same person. This would work, but would be slow as you scale to more people. But it's easy to tell when someone is not a match for any of the known people.
2. You could train any kind of classifier (like an SVM or whatever) like you said. In that case, you would still get a prediction that your unknown person matches one of your known people even if they aren't really a match. But you should get a low confidence score which you can use to determine that the match isn't good enough to consider a real match. So you can use that to know.

It really all depends on your use case. There's other cool stuff you can do like get the encodings for every image in a folder and use the [Chinese Whispers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_Whispers_(clustering_method)) algorithm to automatically cluster every face into groups where each group is one real person (i.e. discover all the individual people in a batch of images).

Hello, this looks like a bug. And the bug is not necessarily the warning. The point is that when you redirect a stream, then 1) that stream shouldn't be fixed by WUC at all, and 2) the readline hook shouldn't be invoked (at least if this hook is input hook).

I'll look at this, but I'm not sure when. The point is WUC has to detect various conditions and behave appropriately – whether the streams are the standard ones and should be fixed, whether something has been redirected, and so on. Some of these checks are in `streams` module. If you want to experiment you can see what the methods of `streams.STDIN` and so on return in your case.

Take a look at the [helpers.py](https://github.com/stephenross/python-mailchimp/blob/bc8a6e255d8e8bf2872fb28dbcb0619ea8f9f318/mailchimp3/helpers.py) file that I have updated. The plan will be to pass anything that should be a subscriber_hash to the `check_subscriber_hash()` function, which will return the value if it is a 32 character hex string (that MD5 generates), and if there is no match, it will attempt to convert it to a hash using `get_subscriber_hash()`. That function will run the string through a regex check to verify that it is a valid email address (no idea on what 0.1% that regex doesn't cover, but I am willing to bet it will be someone that has an awkward email address on purpose and not one that will actually be used). If the regex says the email is a valid address, we compute the MD5 hash and return that, since that is what the API will be expecting. Anywhere where the API expects an email address will use just the regex checking function (`check_subscriber_email()`)

This follows what @charlesthk said, just with a slight reorder:
1. If the value is a valid hash, assume it is correct and return it
2. If the value is not a valid hash, check to see if it is a valid email
3. If no, raise a ValueError, if yes, compute and return the hash

We obviously must trust any hash sent to us that fits the proper MD5 output since we can't verify that an email address was used to compute the hash, but this should resolve any issues where people are trying to throw email addresses at the endpoints that expect hashes, which we can account for using these.

yes, this looks like its an issue with sysmon or Microsoft. seems like invalid data is provided to the event log, or it has become corrupt in some other way. unfortunately, I'm not sure that this python tool can do anything to fix it. i'd recommend registering an exception handler when processing the logs so that you can continue work even if you encounter corrupt entries.

As you mentioned,

> You can see the offending characters in the QNAME field. I am not entirely sure why strange characters are appearing, could be a bug in the DNS server log generator or could well be strange encoding error.

[source](https://github.com/williballenthin/python-evtx/issues/28#issuecomment-264921439)

This is indeed this issue. The log declares a region as ASCII text; however, the data in this field is not valid ASCII. I'm not sure how this comes to be, and its probably a bug in the source of the event log entries.

(Technically, the "ASCII string" fields are stored as single byte character (SBC) strings using a specific code page. In practice, this means ASCII most of the time. I suppose its *possible* that the  weird data is actually valid using in some unexpected-but-valid code page. However, even if we knew the code page, cross-platform Python distributions don't include relevant decoders, so we still wouldn't be able to parse the data.)

Do you need to be able to access the data in these records? If its not a critical requirement, then I'll put together some example code showing how to continue processing after encountering invalid records. Otherwise, I'm going to need to put some thought into how to best expose the raw data for you to parse and handle. My gut reaction is that its not appropriate to hard-code in specialized handlers into this library for the DNS log, but perhaps we could explore what this would look like.

Indeed, `django-sendfile` was not the source of the problem at all. Chrome requires an additional header for seeking to work, see [this StackOverflow post](http://stackoverflow.com/a/8467144/734191).

In the end, my code looked like this:

```
# Ask the web server that is running Django to serve the file
response = sendfile(request, file)

# We add a header to make seeking in videos work in Chrome, see
# http://stackoverflow.com/a/8467144/734191
response['Accept-Ranges'] = 'bytes'
return response
```

Do you need a brief primer of how to use DRF-Mongonengine? I'm writing a post at medium.com about how to use it.

I'm currently using DRF-Mongonengine in combination with Postgres, which is used to store users. (Although, if you want to use DRF's authentication, you can easily migrate to Postgres).

Basically, you need to set up a connection with Mongo database in `settings.py`, you need to create a DRF-Mongoengine router to serve your viewsets and for each view you need to create a triplet (document-serialzier-view).

Connection with database can be established in different ways, depending on which version of Mongoengine you're using. If it's `mongoengine<10` (probably, 0.8.7 or 0.9), than it relies on `pymongo==2`, so you can say: 

```
        mongoengine.connect(
            db=MONGODB_DATABASES['default']['TEST']['name'],
            host=MONGODB_DATABASES['default']['host']
        )
```

Than in `urls.py` you create a router like this:
```
from rest_framework_mongoengine.routers import DefaultRouter
router = DefaultRouter()
router.register(r'tool', ToolViewSet, r"tool")
```
 
And you define your model-serializer-view triplets as follows:

```
from mongoengine import Document
from rest_framework_mongoengine.serializers import DocumentSerializer
from rest_framework_mongoengine.viewsets import ModelViewSet

class Tool(Document):
    label = mf.StringField(required=True)
    description = mf.StringField(required=True, null=True)

class ToolSerializer(DocumentSerializer):
    class Meta:
        model = Tool

class ToolViewSet(ModelViewSet):
    lookup_field =  'id'
    serializer_class = ToolSerializer

Sure, I have a sanity check in my current project where I need to compare sums from two tables against a third table, like this:

    SELECT tab1.id, tab1.value + tmp1.sum - tmp2.sum AS diff FROM tab1
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT ref, SUM(value) AS sum FROM tab2 GROUP BY ref) AS tmp1 ON tab1.id = tmp1.ref
        LEFT JOIN (SELECT ref, SUM(value) AS sum FROM tab3 GROUP BY ref) AS tmp2 ON tab1.id = tmp2.ref
        WHERE tab1.value + tmp1.sum != tmp2.sum`

(The sum fields also need to be COALESCE()d but I've left that out for brevity.)

This is a single query that's blazing fast on the SQL server and returns only the rows that fail the check. But without full support for joinable subselects, I have to run those three `SELECT`s separately, iterate through the whole table in Python and check for mismatches myself. I know from experience that the execution time difference between those two methods can be hours vs. seconds.

``` python
def merge_tablemaps(*maplist):
    """Merge arguments into a single dict, check for name collisions.
    Arguments may be modified in the process."""
    ret = maplist[0]
    for item in maplist[1:]:
        if len(ret) > len(item):
            big, small = ret, item
        else:
            big, small = item, ret
        # Check for name collisions
        for key, val in small.items():
            if big.get(key, val) is not val:
                raise ValueError('Name conflict in table list: %s' % key)
        # Merge
        big.update(small)
        ret = big
    return ret
```

I think the code is behaving as designed although I can see that the way the documentation is split between the `Document` class and context managers makes this confusing.

The documentation you point to says:
> ... a Document object also provides a convenient context manager. This context manager removes having to explicitly fetch() the document ...

and the [context managers documentation](http://python-cloudant.readthedocs.io/en/latest/getting_started.html#context-managers) gives this code example:
```python
# Performs a fetch upon entry and a save upon exit of this block
    with Document(my_database, 'julia30') as doc:
        doc['name'] = 'Julia'
        doc['age'] = 30
        doc['pets'] = ['cat', 'dog', 'frog']
```

so it seems the intent of the document context is always to perform a fetch/save at the start end of the context. Obviously the `fetch` part doesn't make much sense for a document that doesn't yet exist.

I agree that the fact that the `document_id` parameter is marked as optional suggests that the document context should either:
i) suppress that fetch exception when the doc doesn't yet exist
or 
ii) have it's parameters documented separately from the `Document` class, requiring an ID

@marcoabreu Yes, here the benchmark result. My reference implementation is the following LayerNorm that is implemented by stacking broadcasting/reducing operators:

```python
class LayerNormStackSmallOp(HybridBlock):
    """Applies layer normalization to the n-dimensional input array.
    Stack bcast/reduce
    """
    def __init__(self, axis=1, epsilon=1e-5, center=True, scale=True,
                 beta_initializer='zeros', gamma_initializer='ones',
                 in_channels=0, prefix=None, params=None):
        super(LayerNormStackSmallOp, self).__init__(prefix=prefix, params=params)
        self._kwargs = {'eps': epsilon, 'axis': axis}
        self._axis = axis
        self._epsilon = epsilon
        self._center = center
        self._scale = scale
        assert in_channels != 0, "in_channels == 0 is currently not supported"
        if self._center:
            self.gamma = self.params.get('gamma', grad_req='write' if scale else 'null',
                                         shape=(in_channels,), init=gamma_initializer,
                                         allow_deferred_init=True)
        if self._scale:
            self.beta = self.params.get('beta', grad_req='write' if center else 'null',
                                        shape=(in_channels,), init=beta_initializer,
                                        allow_deferred_init=True)

    def moments(self, F, data):
        mean = F.mean(data=data, axis=self._axis, keepdims=True)
        var = F.mean(F.square(F.broadcast_minus(data, mean)),
                     axis=self._axis, keepdims=True)
        return mean, var

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, data, gamma, beta):
        if not self._center and not self._scale:
            return data
        mean, var = self.moments(F, data)
        norm_data = F.broadcast_minus(data, mean)
        norm_data = F.broadcast_mul(norm_data, mx.sym.rsqrt(var + self._epsilon))
        norm_data = F.broadcast_mul(norm_data, gamma)
        norm_data = F.broadcast_add(norm_data, beta)
        return norm_data
```

Note that the library name is based on a hash of the libzmq.so file's contents, so new pyzmq releases will likely have different names. This could create a problem in the situation where you install pyzmq, build your package with -lzmq-XXXX, and then `pip install -U pyzmq` and now it's called libzmq-YYYY.

The purpose of the hashing is to avoid accidental collisions between packages -- if foo.whl bundles libzmq.so.5.1, bar.whl bundles libzmq.so.5.2, and maybe you have a locally compiled package using the system's libzmq.so.5.0, and then you import all those packages into the same Python, you may end up having a very bad time when ld.so picks one of those libzmq.so's at random and then uses it for all three packages.

You could tweak your wheels so that they use a mangled name like `libzmq-for-pyzmq-wheel.so` or something, that's unique but stable across pyzmq releases. (This would need some hacking on auditwheel or whatever, but it's just a python script.) Though, note that you're then effectively adding your bundled libzmq's ABI to your public API, which has consequences you'll probably want to think through.

Also, this simple hack is linux specific -- in particular exporting the bundled libzmq from macos wheels is extraordinarily difficult.

@NeilGirdhar, I'm not sure if this is what you had meant, but `__init_subclass__` does not do what I thought. `__set_name__` is analogous to `class_init`, but `__init_subclass__` is **not** analogous to `subclass_init`.

> Are you saying that because we use @default instead of explicitly requiring the _<trait>_default pattern, we should no longer be accessing underscore names?

No, I mean there's no way to answer the question "what would the dynamic default value have been in my parent class," which is used often in subclasses to modify defaults.

Let's say I have the following:

```python
class Parent(HasTraits):
    some_trait = List(Unicode())
    def _some_trait_default(self):
        return [ os.getcwd() ]
```

In 4.0 and earlier, I could reliably extend this class to add to the default in the child:


```python
class Child(Parent):
    def _some_trait_default(self):
        return super(). _some_trait_default() + ['/']
```

As far as I can tell, this pattern is now impossible via public APIs, because I can no longer determine what the `super.default` would have been without reading private APIs in the source code of the parent class. We need a public way of asking "what would my parent's default value have been?".

I am currently revamping the `luigi.contrib.simulate` module so that it works for every case. I apparently made some wrong asumptions about Luigi's behavior, but I'm currently fixing it. The way it will work is the following:

A unique identifier is stored in a `multiprocessing.Value` so it is shared between all the `RunAnywayTarget`s from the same pipeline execution. The most simple and secure way I found to get a *true* unique identifier is to use the PID of the first target that gets the lock on the value.

The target will then create its temporary files in a directory such as `/tmp/luigi-simulate/<pid>` so there won't be any collision with another pipeline execution, even if it is launched at the same time.

The file deletion is handled in the target's `__init__` and it deletes every folder in `/tmp/luigi-simulate` that's older than 24h. Those two parameters (the temporary path and the 24h) are modifiable by subclassing the target.

@Tarrasch cool. We have a kind of luigi pipeline generator that uses information from a kind of `nx.DiGraph` and then puts together a corresponding luigi pipeline. That's a static part. During the process of putting together luigi tasks, the generator might find that two nodes in the graph are pointing to the same target (a) -> (c) <- (b). In this case, the data from those deps has to be joined in a certain way, and that requires pulling additional data from a source:
(a) -> (c) <- (b)
(src)   _/
 Now, we don't want users to create the (src)->(c) link manually, but be do want the process of pulling the additional data to appear as a proper luigi.Task dependency on the final pipeline. That's where dynamic deps are used: when such situation arises, (c).run() pulls (src) dep dynamically

This looks like a slightly different issue, but it is similar. As I noted in #158, OpaquePacket is dispatched as a last resort when parsing packets if another registered packet class matching the parsed header is not present.

Most of the time this means one of the following things is true:
 - A packet that was parsed is not (yet) implemented in PGPy
 - A parsing bug caused PGPy to get misaligned on the data
 - A packet that was parsed is malformed somehow

In your case, the encrypted packet was parsed correctly, but PGPy did not understand something from the decrypted data. Looking at the output you've provided, I don't see anything that should obviously fail to parse, so I will operate under the assumption that this is probably a parsing bug while I try to reproduce the issue myself.

You'll need to use `get_bucket_notification_configuration` instead.  The [docs](http://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/s3.html?highlight=get_bucket_notification#S3.Client.get_bucket_notification) mention that this is deprecated in favor of `get_bucket_notification_configuration`.

The history on why we have both methods is that we realized that `get_bucket_notification` was modeled incorrectly, and as you mention, we were only parsing one element because we didn't model this value as a list.

The problem we ran into was that we could not modify this in a backwards compatible way (for users that only have one configuration and are using the dictionary without issues).  Because of this, we decided to create a new method, `get_bucket_notification_configuration` which was the "correctly modeled" version of this operation and deprecated the old method.

In some future major version of the SDK (way, way in the future) we'll probably remove `get_bucket_notification`, but for now, we support both methods.

Let me know if you run into any more issues.

Marking as a tentative feature request.  I think this might need a few design iterations and discussion.  While the simplest solution would be to add a `.meta.session`, I do remember we tried to minimize the number of objects that know about the session to simplify code.

I would also be interested in alternative ways to accomplish what you're ultimately after, being able to retrieve create resources using the same session from a given resource.

Here's an idea for a proposed fix.

While the ideal fix would be to hook into the _sending_ side of the upload (the actual socket used by the HTTPConnection) the next best thing we have is to hook into the `read()`s of the file like object we're uploading.  To fix this issue, we need to hook into this process as late as possible such that any other handler/signer that reads the body before sending doesn't affect progress.  One possibility is:

* Add an `enable_callback()`, `disable_callback()` method to the file like object, which indicates whether or not the callback is invoked when `read()`'ing from the file.
* When we wrap the file like object for uploads, we disable callbacks.
* We register a handler for `request-created` via `register_last`, which checks if the object has an `enable_callback` method, and if it does, it will enable callbacks.

This gets around our two problems:

1. By checking for `enable_callback` when we ensure that the client not using the high level interface will not trigger undesired behavior.  This is how we can scope it to a "per request" basis because only requests created by the high level interface will have file like bodies with an `enable_callback` method.
2. By using `register_last`, we ensure we enable callbacks after request signing, which happens in `request-created` via `register()`, and can avoid the double callbacks.

So we have two accounts for _prod_ and _non-prod_. We have some tooling that takes care of creating temporary credentials and writing them to the shared credentials file and updating `AWS_PROFILE`.

For non-prod access:

- I have an IAM user (with access keys) in our non-prod account
- This user is required to authenticate with MFA for API access
- We use STS get-session-token to create temporary non-prod credentials
- These temporary non-prod credentials are written to `~/.aws/credentials`
- My `AWS_PROFILE` is set to `non-prod-mfa` (refer to config below)

These temporary non-prod credentials are picked up from the shared credentials file by various tools like the AWS CLI. As long as those credentials have not expired, I'm not prompted for an MFA code (even by botocore). We're good here.

Now, to get access to the prod account:

- My non-prod user is trusted to assume a particular prod role
- This user is required to authenticate with MFA for API, assume-role access
- We use STS assume-role to elevate access, create temporary prod credentials
- These temporary prod credentials are written to `~/.aws/credentials`
- My `AWS_PROFILE` is set to `prod` (refer to config below)

Again these temporary prod credentials in my shared credentials file are picked up by various tools. As long as the credentials haven't expired, I _should not_ be prompted for an MFA code. Indeed, the AWS CLI picks up these credentials, and I'm able to execute API requests without prompting. This is where botocore does not work as expected, as it prompts me to enter an MFA code.

Concretely, after gaining access to the prod, with a fresh set of prod credentials loaded in my credentials file and my `AWS_PROFILE=prod`, running AWS CLI commands does not prompt me for an MFA code, but botocore does:

```
$ aws iam list-users
{
  "Users": [ ... ]
}
$ python
>>> import boto3, botocore
>>> botocore.session.Session().get_credentials()
Enter MFA code:
```

(To be clear, the interpreter is waiting for user input after `Enter MFA code:`)

I expect `get_credentials()` here to simply grab the appropriate credentials for the given profile from the shared credentials, i.e. something like this:

```
$ python
>>> import boto3, botocore
>>> botocore.session.Session().get_credentials()
<botocore.credentials.Credentials object at 0x105192d90>
>>>
```

As far as I can tell, this behavior is a result of the improper ordering of credential providers: The `SharedCredentialProvider` should take precedence over `AssumeRoleProvider`, and this is actually exactly what the [boto3 documentation declares](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/guide/configuration.html#configuring-credentials).

For reference, a stripped-down AWS config:

```
$ cat ~/.aws/config
[default]
region = us-east-1
output = json

[profile non-prod]

[profile non-prod-mfa]
source_profile = non-prod

[profile prod]
mfa_serial = arn:aws:iam::1234567890:mfa/my-user
role_arn = arn:aws:iam::0987654321:role/prod-user
source_profile = non-prod
```

And my shared credentials file after creating temporary credentials for
both the non-prod and prod accounts:

```
$ cat ~/.aws/credentials
[non-prod]
aws_access_key_id = shh-secret-foo1
aws_secret_access_key = shh-secret-bar1

[non-prod-mfa]
aws_access_key_id = shh-secret-foo2
aws_secret_access_key = shh-secret-bar2
aws_security_token = shh-secret-baz2
aws_session_token = shh-secret-bat2

[prod]
aws_access_key_id = shh-secret-foo3
aws_secret_access_key = shh-secret-bar3
aws_security_token = shh-secret-baz3
aws_session_token = shh-secret-bat3

For each frame `Page Should Contain` searches in, it attempts to locate elements containing the search text. I suspect you have set the implicit timeout to 30 seconds. SeleniumLibrary searched and timed out in 3 frames before finding the element in the 4th frame. 3 x 30 seconds  = 90 seconds + a little overhead.

I see two solutions here:
* if the element (text) is not found in the root frame, set implicit wait to 0 before looking in any other frames
* set implicit wait to 0 before any element search

Pro to 1st solution: acknowledges that implicit wait is also generally used to help sync with the application under test so will be more likely to pass
Con to 1st solution: will wait the implicit wait as a minimum if the text is already present but in a sub-frame.

Pro to 2nd solution: speed
Con to 2nd solution: more likely to break existing tests

I am torn between these choices. Leaning towards the second until I think about what it is like to upgrade and find previously stable tests failing (possibly intermittently) as a result.

Either of these changes risk breaking tests. The recommendation for those who encounter this would be to switch to `Wait Until Page Contains`.

A more radical solution not mentioned above is getting the text of the body element of each frame (guaranteed to exist I believe). I presume there is a reason SL uses XPath and `contains` in lieu of this so only mentioning for completeness.
Closing due inactivity.

Sometimes, also in my application, it's not enough to wait that the element is in the DOM or even visible in the page. This is also true when the application is dynamic by using JavaScript. I did write my own element clicker library, which wait that the element is not in stale state and tries to wait that JavaScript is not anymore running in the background. By checking these two conditions, it did eliminate all the errors to which seemed to be caused by the application not receiving the click.

Unfortunately I can't directly share the library, because it's made on company's time and all code is not written by me. But I can tell how to write one.

The stale state check is based on the selenium explicit waits:  http://selenium-python.readthedocs.io/waits.html#explicit-waits. I am using `element_selection_state_to_be` check but some other check might work better in your case.

The JavaScript running is checked by executing `return jQuery.status` and making sure that the result is zero at least two times in a row.

The above did fixed the problem in my application, but I am not sure will it fix your problem. This is because a silver bullet to fix this type of problem does not exist. It took me several iterations to actually find out what was the reason for the failures. And after the understanding, it came easier to fix the problem in my case. I did have to read the logs from the application, when the no click happens. I did read the console logs from the browser also in same situation. I did capture a video and played it back with slow motion to actually see when element appears in the page and when clicking happens. I did talk to our development and ask what might the reason.

It was a quite a big hunt and required lot of effort to make it work. But it definitely paid off, because now test are more stable.
