Folks may also want to read http://www.curiousefficiency.org/posts/2016/09/python-packaging-ecosystem.html#my-core-software-ecosystem-design-philosophy.

Tightly coupling publishing tools to installation tools is useful for forming an initial tight-knit coherent publishing community, and for exploiting network effects as a commercial platform operator, but it's an approach with a limited lifespan as an ecosystem grows and the diversity of deployment and integration models increases.

For Python, the first phase of that lasted from around 1998->2004 (build with a distutils based setup.py, publish as a tarball if you had no dependencies, or as a Linux distro package if you wanted dependency management), the second phase from around 2004-> 2008 (build with a setuptools based setup.py, install with easy_install), and we're currently still in the third phase (build with a setuptools based setup.py, install with pip).

One key current focus of ecosystem level work is on eliminating the requirement for published projects to include a `setup.py` file at all. Once that's the case, then it would indeed be reasonable for `pipenv` to define a PEP 517 build backend that allowed `Pipfile`-based applications to be published directly to PyPI. It wouldn't involve a `setup.py` file, though, it would be a `pyproject.toml` file that looked something like:

    [build-system]
    requires = ["pipenv"]
    build-backend = "pipenv.publish:build" # Actual API name TBD

Some notes (after poking around with a VM):

* Python for Windows is hopeless in Cygwin, it’s best to ignore it.
* Cygwin Python actually identifies system as POSIX and does the right thing no problem.

So we only need to worry about Msys2 (I don’t want to care about Msys1, but they should be similar). Msys Python works similarly as Cygwin Python, and Pipenv functions correctly out of the box afaict, no problem here.

Python for Windows in Msys is the worst. Python identifies the system as Windows, and even `os.environ['PATH']` is in Windows style. The only thing possible is environment sniffing. There are two options:

* Detect the existence of `MSYSTEM` in environment variables.
* Detect whether there is a command called `cygpath`, and use it in the temporary bashrc if present.

The second option is intriguing, but I think I will go for the first. It is more straightforward and safer.

Finally, I don’t think we need to fix `VIRTUAL_ENV`. The activate file (the Bash one, not the BAT file) uses the Windows notation, so we should as well.

If I’ve learnt any life lessons from this adventure, it’s that both Msys and Cygwin are only work people who know what they are doing. Do not ever teach them to beginners, or they will hit a wall when the abstraction inevitably breaks. If you want Git on Windows, use it in cmd.exe or Powershell. Git Bash is as bad an option as you can get. I’d choose WSL all day every day.

Yeah, I knew that he was involved with Cargo, which seems like it may be evidence that we need to consider that choice, since Cargo was made well after that post was written. Since he knew the benefits and pitfalls of using it in Ruby at the time, his choice to not replicate it for Rust means something to me.

It makes sense to me that the compilation nature of Rust could change the math, but I haven't come up with _how_ it changes it. It seems like virtually all of the reasoning that we have behind doing the three-file approach still applies, even to Rust.

My own reasoning at the moment really wonders if having `Cargo.toml` fill both the slot of `pypackage.toml` and `Pipfile` is actually a bad choice. In my own limited work with Rust, I've created a library that included a reference implementation binary that _used_ that library. The library shouldn't have the lock file checked in, but the binary reasonably should. So I had to choose, and had to go with keeping the lock file out of the repository so I didn't screw up the usage of it as a library. I could make them separate repositories, but that didn't seem worthwhile.

Thus, my own thinking makes me think that the split is a good thing. I wonder if we could get @wycats to comment on the factors that lead to the decision that Cargo made.

Perhaps for Cargo several of the things that are constraints for us were deemed unnecessary. If enough of our reasons for keeping them separate were deemed of enough less utility for Cargo, then the overhead of an extra file may have been enough to decide to take the bad with the good, and skip the added file.

@tigran-a `consumer.consume()` actually [does not](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/e3fb2e95ff29d9bfcb080991ad6dcbf140a71ac9/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L357) communicate with the server in any way. The consumer periodically runs `fetch()` on a worker thread that gets messages from the broker and populates an internal queue with those messages. `consume()` then consumes that queue one message at a time. This means that there is a difference between the messages you've seen from `consume()` and the messages that pykafka has actually fetched from the broker at the time that you reinstantiate the consumer. However, the offset committed to kafka is that of the last message returned from `consume()`, so I'm still unable to put my finger on a circumstance that enables the error you're seeing. 
One thing that strikes me when looking at your reconnect code is that you're allowing `self.consumer` to persist until the very end of the reconnect logic. The consumer instance holds a reference to the internal `Cluster` instance - I don't see any direct ways this could cause problems, but it's at least something I think you don't mean to be doing.
More broadly - I wonder what happens if you remove as much of your reconnect code as possible. There's a lot of state that gets destroyed locally, on zookeeper, and on the kafka broker when you stop the client once every second. Though I can't discern exactly what's happening, I bet you'll have better luck if you reconnect less frequently, or only in response to exceptions from the consumer. To my knowledge, all of the common errors in the consumer now result in exceptions being raised to the caller's thread, so you should have no problem simply waiting to reconnect when they're caught.
Please post here if you make any headway on this. Thanks!

Your diff comment indirectly highlights a problematic aspect of this PR (and the only one I can see): changing the return type from an integer array to a float array will break any code that uses it for indexing. I don't know if this is a common use case -- of course, this is what you have to do if you want to find the intensity of the image at the center pixel of the blob, but considering that blobs may have arbitrarily complicated topography, that particular intensity doesn't seem like a very interesting property (if you have a nice and well-defined peak, you will probably use `peak_local_max()`, which still returns an integer array).

A different solution could be to return two separate arrays: `local_maxima[:, :2]`, which is still integer, and `sigma_list[local_maxima[:, 2]]`, with floating-point sigma values. However, I imagine breaking the return signature is worse than changing the return type. In addition, I'm thinking about submitting a feature request (which I'll consider working on if anyone is interested) for (optional) sub-pixel precision in peak and blob detection. If this is implemented, `local_maxima` could be a float array anyway, so splitting the return signature wouldn't make sense.

In any case, truncating sigma values to integers is plainly incorrect, and it would be great to find a way to avoid this.

@mehditlili and @jni, has 16-bit reading/writing been addressed in imageio?

For many image formats, imageio is a wrapper for Pillow, so it comes with all of the same limitations including not being able to load multichannel (e.g., RGB) 16-bit PNGs. The only way to do that is via the freeimage backend, which requires loading/installing a separate dependency: https://github.com/imageio/imageio/issues/204

Hi @anrie! Thanks for taking time to review.

In fact, all of those concerns are already addressed. Maybe we could just update the docs. I've already isolated all calls to methods of `easy_thumbnails`, and those are now behind the `Backend` facade.

The `cropped_thumbnail` [delegates the version generation to the backend](https://github.com/jonasundderwolf/django-image-cropping/pull/92/files#diff-7e1495c729879e73dd167bdaa65fd6c1R91).

The user can register any custom class as a backend by overriding the default settings. The `IMAGE_CROPPING_BACKEND` expects a dotted path to a class that implements the required methods of a Backend:

``` python
IMAGE_CROPPING_BACKEND = 'image_cropping.backends.fb.FileBrowserBackend'
```

As an aditional layer of configuration, the user can provide an optional dict that will be used to populate the constructor of the backend, as in:

``` python
IMAGE_CROPPING_BACKEND_PARAMS = {'version_suffix': 'image_cropping'}
```

There's an Abstract Base Class to show the [required methods of a custom backend](https://github.com/jonasundderwolf/django-image-cropping/pull/92/files#diff-7367b8d7a54718a53906b6ac784f37c9R9).

The `FileBrowserBackend` is an optional backend self-contained in a single file that is only imported if specified in the `IMAGE_CROPPING_BACKEND` setting. I think that it is a good showcase on "how to write a custom backend".

Would it really be overkill? Haven't we all encountered a bug in production, usually a type error of some kind, where you look at the trace and you're like, "I have no idea how this even happened." And it's one of those errors that occurs rarely, under particular circumstances, and is therefore difficult to reproduce. (It is in part difficult to reproduce because the conditions of the error are hidden!)

It is in those instances when I wish I had more clues. If I had the locals at each stack frame, it might clue me in on what the real issue is. In fact, just as I was in the middle of writing this, one of my coworkers asked me about a bug. We use a service's API, and it's giving a weird error for one particular user -- just one, not any of the other millions of users we have. There's a variable two frames down from where the error is raised that I wish I had the value of, but I don't.

I agree, however, that the size of the payload is a concern. Perhaps we could do something like, truncate the rendered string of a variable if it is too long. For example, if you have a variable which is a 10 KB string, we only print the first 30 characters. That sort of thing.

In any case, it could be up to the individual project/application to decide how they might curtail what information is sent. Modifying the API to accept local variables on each stack frame would be a prerequisite step.

In our current system we do access rights by a lot of _collection juggling_. That is, even today we have access rights in the record (sort of), however in a quite obscured way as one needs to fiddle out what rights result from the intersection of the collections exposed in the bibliographic. Indeed, even today this results in quite a bunch of versions that result only from fixing access rights. (Point is, that in our setups at join² it is a requirement that records are _not_ publicly available till they reach a certain curation stage.)

Right now, all this is done by using `websubmit` with quite a bit of program logic that is not accessible to the cataloguers + the restriction to most of the cataloguers to use `websubmit` only to ensure all rights are handled properly.

Given this, I follow @egabancho argument. I would argue that access rights should live in the record from the start in a clear manner, replacing the current collection handling by an easier readable method. Furthermore I do not see the drawbacks outlined by @egabancho as such grave issues while I quite agree with the benefits he pointed out above. Even a history of changes that stem only from access right handling is probably not that much of an drawback. Probably the largest issue is the outlined forced compliance with collections, however this should happen anyway.

Concerning serialization: if one want to give cataloguers an easy view and access to the ACLs I think mapping it to the master format is imperative. From our experience I'd strongly argue to give cataloguers access to the ACL. (Maybe this can be restricted to a sub set of specially privileged cataloguers, if in doubt, but it should not require programmers or admins.) In fact we wrote quite a bit of `websubmit`-code to make this sort of possible. We'd happily drop this code ;)

Some thoughts:

- What happens on exchange of records? Ie. how to ensure that rights are propagated properly. In 1.x `InstallInvenio` ensures in join² that all partners have the _same collections_ with the _same access_ rights to them. As access is driven by collections we just leave them untouched thus the records get proper rights. If I understand @egabancho proposal correctly this would not be necessary anymore and everything would just work. I'd consider this an advantage.
- One should probably consider using sort of templates for ACL assignments. To hook it up with cataloguing tools for cataloguers a simple association of an authority record comes to mind. E.g. I could well imagine enhanding `540` by the currently still missing `$0` to assign an authority ID. Effectively, this linked record can contain all the technical stuff like static `firerole` definitions.
- One should consider to handle files similarly to the bibliographic. E.g. if one goes for som 5xx in Marc to handle bibliographic ACL one should consider going for holdings in 8xx instead of `bibdocfile` to handle files and ACLs.
In B2Share case, all of them. We only use marc21 for exports, not internally. Especially the "deposits" mapping which prevent us from using the same name.

From what I understand, a token 'expires' when it is older than `JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA`.

You can refresh a _non-expired_ token until it is older than `JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA `.

So if `JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA` < `JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA`, then the `JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA` setting is (in a practical sense) superseded by  `JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA`.

That is what I've understood from the docs and from playing with the library. Why are these the defaults?

    JWT_EXPIRATION_DELTA = datetime.timedelta(seconds=300)
    JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA = datetime.timedelta(days=7)

You'll never be able to refresh after 4 days because the token will have expired! Is there any point to the `JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRATION_DELTA` setting?

Through `unittest`'s `Mock` we can specify the class as a type, rather than as a string. As `autoclass` returns a class we can then do something like: `Mock(spec=autoclass('generated.MyClass'))`, where `generated.MyClass` is a Java Class.

We can thus close this :)

As an extra mile, I find this topic interesting for this library, as mocking is a way of preventing excessive trips to the JVM in unit tests.

Hi @dacoex, what you're asking for is almost possible now.  `show_grid` is a convenience function, and in the latest version of qgrid you can use the widget object directly to render a qgrid, like so:
```
from IPython.display import display
grid = qgrid.QGridWidget(df=spy)
display(grid) #alternative to show_grid
```
This would allow you get the underlying DataFrame object using `grid.df` and then you could pass it into your plot function as you did with `df_new` in your example.  The only problem left is I doubt that your plotting library would automatically pick up on the edits to the DataFrame until you rerun the cell to generate the plot again.  So I think you'll find that after you make edits, you'll need to execute the cell with the `scatter` function again to re-plot the DataFrame.  

I'm sure it's possible to automate that but it would be specific to the plotting library.  For that reason I don't think it's a problem that would be worthwhile for this project, so I'm closing this as fixed.

Most of the postgres parameters will be written into postgresql.conf and it will be possible to change them  with "alter system". wal_keep_segments is still not among these parameters, but we'll provide alternative: it would be possible to change parameters globally, for all members of the cluster (by changing value of 'config' key in DCS) or change parameters in the patroni.yml and send SIGHUP to the Patroni process (or by making "reload" API call). Patroni will get new configuration and apply it to the postgres by performing pg_ctl reload. If some changes require restart it will set "restart_pending" flag which is exposed via REST API and also written into member key in a DCS. Restart is a manual task, but there in an API call for that. Also restart could be performed with patronictl.

A requests Session object also provides its own connection pooling.

The only negative of using a `Session` is that connections made within the memento_client will not be pooled with connections made using requests outside of the memento_client.

I believe it is quite unlikely the code calling memento_client will also be accessing the timegate/timemap servers directly -- the calling code should use memento_client for any requests to the timegate/timemap servers.

However the calling code might wish to use connections to the original uri's, so there will be some opportunity to re-use those connections.

A way to allow good results in all cases is that the `MementoClient` constructor could take an optional `session` parameter, so that the calling code can allow `MementoClient` to share its existing session, which it will close at an appropriate time.  If the caller doesnt provide a session, `MementoClient` would create its own.

> Are you sure there is not an alternative course of action for Pywikibot?

We would need to dig into the internals of `requests`.  e.g. we might manually close the connections which are no longer needed and should be pro-actively closed.  We like connection pooling of the requests to the timegate, however the resources consumed by keeping connections to the original urls need to be released as weblinkchecker.py spawns many threads with many connections and thus needs unneeded connections to be closed.

There may be some `requests` flags that we can set that helps weblinkchecker to pin the timegate connection open but pro-actively close other connections.  I'll look into this.

As for why it did not solve the challenge, the issue is actually related to the script (which caused a MemoryException) and not a bug in Manticore itself. 

One mistake with the script was the use of a global variable to hold buffer_addr, instead of using `m.context` (or the recently added `state.context`).  Because multiprocessing was enabled, the process that executed the "win" hook may not be the one where buffer_addr was assigned in! The other issue with the script was an incorrect understanding of what RAX points to.

See below for a working solution script (works on manticore master):


```
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 --

from manticore import Manticore


m = Manticore('ais3_crackme', ['a'*30])

buffer_addr=0
num_bytes=30

@m.hook(0x4005cd)
def hook(state):
    print("fake 2 args EDI=2")
    state.cpu.EDI=0x2


@m.hook(0x4005f3)
def hook(state):
    print("retreive buffer from rax")
    global buffer_addr
    # print state.cpu.read_int(state.cpu.RAX), 'yoo'
    # assert 0
    solution = state.new_symbolic_buffer(num_bytes)
    state.constrain(solution[0] == ord('a'))
    state.constrain(solution[1] == ord('i'))
    state.constrain(solution[2] == ord('s'))
    state.constrain(solution[3] == ord('3'))
    state.constrain(solution[4] == ord('{'))
    buffer_addr=state.cpu.read_int(state.cpu.RAX)
    state.context[1] = buffer_addr
    print 'yo buff addr', hex(buffer_addr)
    print state.cpu.read_bytes(buffer_addr, 8)
    print ("buffer addr : %08x " %(buffer_addr))
    state.cpu.write_bytes(buffer_addr, solution)

@m.hook(0x40060e)
def hook(state):
    print("fail path ....")
    state.abandon()

@m.hook(0x4005ae)
def hook(state):
    print ("++++ hook @0x4005ae <+142>: movzx  eax,BYTE PTR [rax] .....")
    print (state.cpu.RAX)

@m.hook(0x4005b1)
def hook(state):
    print ("++++ hook @0x4005b1 <+145>: test   al,al .....")

@m.hook(0x400602)
def hook(state):
    print("it is a win path")
    buffer_addr = state.context[1]
    print 'yo buff addr', hex(buffer_addr)
    print state.cpu.read_bytes(buffer_addr, 8)
    res = ''.join(map(chr, state.solve_buffer(buffer_addr,num_bytes)))
    #res=''
    #for i in xrange (num_bytes):
    #    res+=res+''.join(chr(state.solve_one(i)))
    print("flag is : %s"%(res))
    m.terminate()

m.verbosity =1
m.run(procs=10)
```

This is part of a KDP algorithm, where phase texture is used as a identifier for bad gates. The entire KDP algorithm, which makes 3 passes per ray, runs in 2 seconds or less on my 4-yo laptop when analyzing a busy radar volume, so the texture part is maybe only a third of that cost.

Anyway, this seemed germane to the discussion, and might be useful as a template for implementing fast texture processing in Py-ART.

If you want to have secure application, then never keep your secrets in the memory due to the [heart bleed attacks](https://heartbleed.com/). Hence, the `OAuth2Session` should not keep the `client_secret` in the memory. The solution are:
1. Secure only: To pass the `get_client_secret` function as the `__init__` parameter, so the session would call `get_client_secret()` to get the secret.
2. Secure and insecure: To have a `client_secret` parameter which may be function or string. And the doc would strongly discourage to pass the string directly. Then it's up to the user to decide if she wants (needs) secure or insecure version.

## BigtableTableAdmin (2/6 custom)

1. `CreateTable` -> `"v2/{parent=projects/*/instances/*}/tables"`
1. `ListTables` -> `"v2/{parent=projects/*/instances/*}/tables"`

1. `DropRowRange` -> `"v2/{name=projects/*/instances/*/tables/*}:dropRowRange"`

@rimey The APIs document that the timestamps being pushed across the wire are in UTC:

Datastore
---------

- [Timestamp values in datastore entries](https://cloud.google.com/datastore/reference/rest/v1beta3/projects/runQuery#Value.FIELDS.timestamp_value) require RFC 3339 "Zulu" format.

Storage
-------

- [`Bucket.timeCreated`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/buckets#timeCreated) and [`Bucket.updated`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/buckets#updated) are not user modifiable, and are always returned as RFC 3339 Zulu format.
- [`Object.timeCreated`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects#timeCreated), [`Object.timeDeleted`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects#timeDeleted), and [`Object.updated`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects#updated) are also not user-modifiable, and are always returned as RFC 3339 Zulu format.

Pubsub
------

- If passed, [`PubsubMessage.publishTime`](https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/reference/rest/v1/PubsubMessage#FIELDS.publish_time) is required to be in RFC 3339 Zulu format at nanosecond precision (annoyingly, it is sometimes returned truncated, but it always has the `Z`).

BigQuery
--------

- [Fields of type `TIMESTAMP](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/preparing-data-for-bigquery#dataformats) are converted to Unix epoch values with microsecond precision.  Input values which are floats are assumed to already be UTC;  likewise string input values without explicit zones.  String input values with explicit zones are converted to UTC before storage.  The API returns TIMESTAMP values as microseconds since the UTC epoch.

Logging
-------

- [`LogEntry.timestamp`](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/api/ref_v2beta1/rest/v2beta1/LogEntry#FIELDS.timestamp) is required to be RFC 3339 Zulu, to nanosecond precision.

DNS
---

- [`Changes.startTime`](https://cloud.google.com/dns/api/v1/changes#startTime) is defined as " RFC3339 text format."  Because we don't have system tests running yet for it, I'm not positive that it includes the Zulu suffix.

They aren't being phased out and they aren't going anywhere.

We've discussed in our meetings that they be stateless and are simply a way to make HTTP requests to the service.

-----

**Datastore**: We allow `connection` as an optional argument. Using implicitly set connections, a user can happily use the library without needing to know what a connection is.

This was spurred on by comments that there were "too many classes". We realized that we didn't need the typical user to worry about a `Connection` (beyond setting the right environment variable for credentials).

I ran some speed tests on a p2.xlarge on GPU with a variety of setups, here are the graphs:

* The green and orange lines correspond to baselines in master, with the commit ID in brackets.
* Red and blue are the batched topK implementations that @fhieber referenced above.
* The purple line is a two-step topK explained below.

## Split by batch size

![speedbybatch](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6504048/38606829-71bfad28-3d77-11e8-83b4-e4337d89690b.png)


## Split by beam size

![speedbybeam](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6504048/38606838-753cc706-3d77-11e8-95c7-9f222dad2889.png)

## Discussion

* It seems that on GPU the batched topK implementations don't negatively affect the batch=1 use case, as was expected.
* Batched topK brings nice speed-ups for most batch-beam size combinations, with some coming in roughly the same as the baseline.
* Mxnet and numpy batched topK versions are not terribly different, with the mxnet version performing better for `beam_size>5` and the numpy version for `beam_size<5`.
* The batched topK versions show a nearly linear speed-up as batch sizes increases in the greedy-case (batch=1). 

This last point led me to think that the bottleneck in the topK was the larger vocabulary dimension, which wasn't scaling well. For larger beam sizes we'd be performing topK row-wise on a matrix of size `(batch_size, beam_size * vocab_size)`, where adding more rows (i.e. sentences) scaled well as seen in the graphs for beam_size=1. So I tried the purple line, which is a two-step topK: first run topK row-wise on the matrix `(batch_size * beam_size, vocab_size)`, so we'd end up with the top k entries for each *hypothesis*. Then, run another topK on the reshaped result, which would only be `(batch_size, k*k)`. Unfortunately, this didn't show much improvement.

All in all, it seems the batched topK operators work well on GPU decoding, leaning towards the mxnet one. We should check there's no degradation on CPU for non-batched decoding.

Also, I did some rough calculations on `channels_first` and `channels_last` performance and by how much the performace improved if `broadcast_add` operator gives similar performance on  `channels_first` as compared to `channels_last`.

MXNet channels_first takes 214 sec/epoch(From above analysis)

From profiling, I got rough estimation that one loop takes 350 msec(see below diagram).

![image2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12561286/39272339-7c3bb796-4890-11e8-9176-90f0ad61ad20.png)


So in 1 epoch, this model loop runs. `214 sec / 350 msec = 611 = 600(Approx.)`
It run 600 times if we ignore initialization and termination time.

In one loop, the amount of time taken by `broadcast_add()` operator when data is `channels_first` is 

**Forward prop: 3.8 msec**

**Backward prob: 47.9 msec**

**Total: 51.7 msec**

In one loop, the amount of time taken by `broadcast_add()` operator when data is `channels_last` is 

**Forward prop: 2.5 msec**

**Backward prob: 23.1 msec**

**Total: 25.6 msec**

If we assume that the `broadcast_add()` gives similar performance on  `channels_first` and `channels_last`, then the amount of time to execute one loop is `350 - 51.7 + 25.6 = 323.9`. 

So, the amount of time took by the model in one epoch is:

OLD: 600 * 350 = 210 sec

NEW: 600 * 323 = 194 sec

We get roughly 16 sec boost in performance if  `broadcast_add()` gives similar performance on  `channels_first` and `channels_last`.

Note: This analysis is done on `keras/example/cifar10_cnn.py` and the configurations is same as my above post.

Ok, I see what you're trying to do now.

A few serious problems:

1. There's no documented analysis of the situation and the proposed fix. Changes are initiated by proposing an enhancement (using an enhancement proposal, aka. analysis page). There's a lot to discuss and resolve before getting to work, foremost of which is the API additions/changes that will be made since those are very difficult to change once set.

2. There are not acceptance or unit tests. All successful merges include tests.

3. You're making destructive changes at the highest possible level. You shouldn't make destructive changes (unless asked for by the user) and you should make changes as locally as possible. Removing the `w:hyperlink` element changes the document by essentially removing all hyperlinks (and the other information they contain). It's even worse than this (relationship links are left broken, etc.), but this is enough. This makes it a non-starter.

4. You're doing this by modifying `parse_xml()`, which is a fundamental function used throughout the library. This incurs risk in every module that uses it, which is many. Wrong use of a broadly used function.

5. You've added an unwelcome dependency. We don't use `six` on this project. See the `compat` module to see how we take care of those things.

So perhaps you understand my reply better now. This is the wrong way to fix the issue, in a lot of ways.

Sorry I'm a bit late to the party. The fact that I wrote the events code doesn't mean that I know how it works!

Sonos units do indeed continue trying to send events for some time after an unsuccessful attempt.  You can see this at http://{device_ip}:1400/status/opt/log/anacapa.trace.  Eventually they will give up and drop the subscription.

I don't see any real harm in setting a short timeout in the unsubscribe method, since we don't really care out whether the request is successful or not. It is possible that an error will be raised by the sonos device, but it will drop the subscription in due course anyway. I don't know that we need the timeout to be configurable, but again it doesn't do any harm.  (We need to be careful about all the timeout variables though - there are now quite a few and it might be confusing.)

At some point, we might go full async (perhaps using gevent, which finally supports Python 3, and  https://github.com/kennethreitz/grequests )

Why is it not being addressed? Time. All of us only have so many hours in a day, and with all of us doing stuff in our spare time, sometimes fall to the way-side.

Ultimately, all the tests that are failing are because of spec changes that the testsuite has been updated to match but html5lib-python hasn't been. If it were down to me alone, I wouldn't have updated the submodule reference (and as such we'd appear to be passing everything). There's undoubtedly merit to being always up to date with expectations stored in our own repo, but I'd wait until all the code was written for that before updating the submodule reference.

At the end of the day, the combination of the HTML parsing algorithm now being widely implemented makes html5lib less interesting as an experimental implementation, and as a generic HTML parsing library it works in the vast majority of cases, so there's just less motivation than there once was.

Is the PR dead? Well, yes. I probably should've commented in there a while ago. Really we need something that not only has expected failures but also a way to keep them up to date; I think we're not far off having that done (but this is the case with many things around here — 99% done, but not quite!).

I know back when everything was in one repo we used to frequently have tests failing — and we just relied on the number of failures remaining constant as a sign we weren't breaking stuff (and yes, I know that doesn't really work), because at the end of the day updating the parser to the spec is in general a lot of tedious work (patches very much welcome, and far easier to do well than rewriting our test harness code to add expected failures and the like!).

Another approach in these situations is to write some simple helper functions that are dead easy to understand and which remove code from the confusing section.  So you might instead have e.g.,

```python
EMPTY = set()

def _identicalMatch(a, b):
    return a == b and len(AMBIGUOUS[a]) == 1

def _ambiguousMatch(a, b, matchAmbiguous):
    return (matchAmbiguous and
            AMBIGUOUS.get(a, EMPTY) & AMBIGUOUS.get(b, EMPTY))
```

and in the section in question for the ticket you'd then just have the crystal clear:

```python
if _identicalMatch(a, b):
    identicalMatchCount += 1
elif _ambiguousMatch(a, b, matchAmbiguous):
    ambiguousMatchCount += 1
else:
    nonGapMismatchCount += 1
```

You'd have to convince yourself (tests!) that the above was correct.  Also note that `_ambiguousMatch` is called only if `_identicalMatch` fails. That's important because `_ambiguousMatch` also returns `True` for two identical non-ambiguous bases. You could make it more explicit:

```python
def _ambiguousMatch(a, b, matchAmbiguous):
    return (matchAmbiguous and
            (len(AMBIGUOUS.get(a, EMPTY)) > 1 or len(AMBIGUOUS.get(b, EMPTY)) > 1) and
            AMBIGUOUS.get(a, EMPTY) & AMBIGUOUS.get(b, EMPTY))
```

or

```python
def _ambiguousMatch(a, b, matchAmbiguous):
    return (matchAmbiguous and
            not _identicalMatch(a, b) and
            AMBIGUOUS.get(a, EMPTY) & AMBIGUOUS.get(b, EMPTY))
```

A small downside of helper functions is that they sometimes repeat work, but that's usually not a problem unless you're really trying to optimize for speed. Usually it's better to optimize for understanding.

I agree with you, though I think `find()` mostly doesn't apply to predefined objects, since `PanObject.find()` is designed to iteratively and recursively find a PanObject under a PanObject.  Typically, children of a PanObject are stored in a list so that makes sense to handle it iteratively and recursively.  But since there are sooo many predefined objects, and they all have different names, and there are no sub-objects, this method of finding doesn't really apply.  It makes more sense to store them in a dict for easy reference.  So a 'find' would be very easy because you just pull it up by name from the dict where the name is the key and the PanObject is the value.  The dict could be hidden behind methods to access it in different ways (like a `find` method) but the operations performed would be on the dict of objects, not on a `children` list.

I don't think there's an easy way to fix that issue, unfortunately.  We also have more than just "unit tests" running in TeamCity - there are sniff tests and other things, which definitely need network access.  One option is to mark any such test as flaky, and that should dramatically lower the probability of these sporadic failures.

The `cuda_device` parameter that's passed to `predict_json` only affects what device the _input tensors_ get created on.  It doesn't affect where the model lives.  You are calling `predict_json` programmatically, instead of with our command line tools, and you've probably forgotten to call `model.cuda(cuda_device)` on the model that you've passed to that predictor.

That should fix your particular issue, but to fix this for future users, we should do one of the following:

1. Improve the documentation for `Predictor.predict_json`, `Model.forward_on_instance`, and anywhere else that's relevant, saying that the `cuda_device` flag there only affects the input tensors, not the model, and the model device needs to match.
2. If the input tensors need to match the device the model is on, and we're calling `forward_on_instance` _from the model_, shouldn't we already know what device to use for creating input tensors?  Why do we need the `cuda_device` argument for these methods at all?  Seems like it should be easy to remove it.

To address point 2:

# Multiple Python Environments

- tox automates and standardizes testing in python using virtual environments
- virtualenv creates isolated python environments

Your choice is to depend on the above tools, create your own scripts, or rely on a particular CI tool.  I think there is one way to go forward without relying on CI infra.

## The above tools
Becomes an implementation detail that can be used in any CI environment.  Those that have used tox before will be able to immediately understand how things work, those that haven't just have to type the command tox.

## Your own tools
Becomes an implementation detail that can be used in any CI environment.  The scripts will be unfamiliar to others and will require people to read the code or read the docs.  It is unclear how you automate testing with multiple python environments with current scripts without relying on CI infrastructure.

## CI infrastructure
Give in, tie yourself to a CI platform:
- [TravisCI multiple envs](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/languages/python)
- [CircleCI multiple envs](https://circleci.com/docs/language-python)

# Operating System Environments
If you are also concerned with operating system environments, then this is something to consider:

## using docker
You are essentially only testing in linux, but have support for all platforms because of docker compatibility on all platforms.  You also have a ground truth environment for reproducing errors (this is really less important for this package since it has no external binary dependencies).  You don't have to set environment variables in CI platforms for the OS;  Then again you are not proving that you are providing native support for anything but linux.  If someone wants to run their tests they can run the python script, but there is a hidden docker dependency here and they have to make sure that it is properly installed on their machine.

*If you only care about providing native support for linux, then I could see you going this direction.*

## not using docker
You can prove that you have native support on OSX and linux with Travis or Circle (requires two configuration lines, hardly ties you to a CI framework) and Windows support with Appveyor.  Furthermore, an individual contributor could run tox natively with confidence, because you have tested for those three scenarios.  They also don't have to deal with the hidden docker dependency. (tox dependency `pip install tox` = less work than [ensuring docker is installed](https://docs.docker.com/engine/installation/))

*If you care about natively supporting OSX, Linux, and Windows, I would suggest this way forward.*

## My Questions

- What would you like to see from me so that I can create an acceptable PR that will incorporate pre-commit and testing?
- Do you want me to run the tox command in docker?
- Do you want me to run tox outside of docker and also run the docker test?
- Do you want me to not use tox at all and stick to creating a script that can be run in the bin directory?

Because Deis remains is not multi-tenant, in practice Deis was unaffected. It might have been possible for code to be run while fetching a buildpack, but at that point the user is privileged and could just run arbitrary code within their app anyways. And slugbuilder is an isolated Docker container, so impact was minimal anyways.

But the real lesson here is that please treat `git clone` and fetching/checking out users' repos as untrusted. [CVE-2015-7545](https://access.redhat.com/security/cve/cve-2015-7545) affected recursive fetching of submodules and [CVE-2014-9390](https://developer.atlassian.com/blog/2014/12/securing-your-git-server/) would have been terrible except everyone's servers run on Linux. So just please continue to do `git clone` within an isolated environment, and carefully consider git if you ever decide to make Deis multi-tenant.

Meaning, if I:

```console
git submodule add git@github.com:ianblenke/otherproject.git otherproject/
```

you will see a .gitmodules file like:

```
[submodule "otherproject"]
	path = otherproject
	url = git@github.com.ianblenke/otherproject.git
```

The key point of using a submodule for otherproject/ isn't merely to have a link to a foreign repo, it is to track a specific SHA1 of the foreign repo.

This SHA1 is present in even a shallow clone of .git, as the folder reference otherproject/ is actually stored in the git tree leaf as the SHA1 itself.

Disambiguating the remote origin of the otherproject/ folder is the function of the .gitmodule file.

I too once thought as you, that the .gitmodule somehow magically encapsulated the entirety of submodules. Unfortunately, this is not the case.

This is why I need a shallow clone of the .git folder. My developers work from features branches and need to track the SHA1 of submodule folders for associated projects that are also implementing associated feature branches. When they deploy these feature branches to an environment as a deis application, it is critical that the submodules be tied to the SHA1 present in the shallow .git tree, so as not to track the master branch by default.

Some questionable items are surfacing as I chug through this.
Lots of them result from the create/update duality. Those options look *very* similar, but change what is and is not required.

- Endpoint Creation
```shell
$ globus transfer endpoint create \
    --endpoint-type [globus-connect-personal|globus-connect-server|s3] ...
```
Should endpoint-type really become a positional? Or should it become truly optional, defaulting to `globus-connect-server` or `globus-connect-personal`?
A positional Choice type seems really awkward/weird to me.

What about `display_name`? Right now it's required on creation, but that mismatches with endpoint update. (Same holds for shared endpoints).

- Role Creation
```shell
$ globus transfer endpoint role create ENDPOINT_ID \
    --role ... \
    --principal ... \
    --principal-type ...
```

All four of those things are required, but asking people to memorize the order seems like a bit of a stretch. Maybe these are better off as required options?

- ACL Add
```shell
$ globus transfer endpoint acl add-rule ENDPOINT_ID:PATH \
    --permissions ... \
    --principal ... \
    --principal-type ...
```

As above, this seems like too many.
It also raises the common pairing of principal-type and principal value. Perhaps something like the `ENDPOINT_ID:PATH` notation would be appropriate for those?
It's hard for me to make an argument for `group(<UUID>) | identity(<UUID>)` when this is a problem with a general and natural solution when we consider that we own our own URN space.

- ACL Update
```shell
$ globus transfer endpoint acl update-rule ENDPOINT_ID RULE_ID PERMISSIONS
```
This seems fine, given that we go with a high-level identifier, a more specific identifier, and then a value.
However, it should presumably match up well with `acl add-rule`. Does that argue for making it `--permissions` (required), or `PERMISSIONS` for both?

- Endpoint Server Add
```shell
$ globus transfer endpoint server add ENDPOINT_ID \
    --hostname ...
```
This looks fine to change to `HOSTNAME`, but it's kind of weird to write `HOSTNAME --scheme gsiftp` -- the order on that looks wrong.
We could accept `gsiftp://HOSTNAME` and `ftp://HOSTNAME`, with a default of `gsiftp` when no scheme is given. Does that work well?
We also need to consider `globus transfer endpoint server update`.

I'm removing the question label. I think the most reasonable thing we can do for v1 is to make the helptext much shorter. Going with the originally suggested text -- after consideration, it satisfies basic documentation needs and there's nothing obviously better.

As a follow up, it is possible for us to hide this option on all help except for top-level `globus --help`, but I don't know if that's necessary.

I think just doc  its behavior at this point. We have on our backlog to add much richer, server-side globbing/ expression support, but probably not until later this year.

Sorry for the many conflated issues.  Here's an explanation:

1. PREFIX/Library/bin was not put on PATH by old installers (Anaconda < 2.4; Miniconda <3.18).  This led to Matplotlib and/or spyder not loading, because the Qt dlls were not found.
2. conda-env-2.4.4-1 was my approach to add Library/bin to path.  There was a mistake in it that made it work only on Python3 base installations: https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/509#issuecomment-154517037
3. @marscher recommended using site.py instead of trying to modify PATH: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/1753#issuecomment-154421628 - this will avoid the troubles with the batch script.  The downside is that any additional executables (hdf5 tools, for example) will not be directly accessible from the root environment, if that root environment was installed with an old installer, and you have not otherwise added PREFIX/Library/bin to your PATH.

I am presently building and testing fixes.  I hope to have these out later today.

One issue with all of this is that the passive way that options are handled. That is, we are assuming that the package somehow _figures out_ that `nice_feature1` is enabled simply because `package1` and `package2` have been installed. There is no mechanism here for _telling_ the package what options have been selected.

For anyone running into this issue, the latest version of the CLI fixes this but your `~/.azure/azureProfile.json` file is likely already invalid. This is why purging `~/.azure` resolves this.

---
Details:
The original bug was that 'cloudName' was saved to ~/.azure/azureProfile.json instead of 'environmentName'. So when loading the file, the CLI would fail with `KeyError: 'environmentName'`.

The PR linked in this issue now correctly saves 'environmentName' to the file instead of 'cloudName'. But if your azureProfile.json already contains the incorrect 'cloudName', you'll see the error.

1. Generally the errors bubble up from the service, especially since the SDKs (being autogenerated) have very little ability to add client side validation that is business logic relevant. We do this on the CLI side when the service fails to enforce its business logic and it creates a negative experience for customers. Another reason we tend to not do it on the CLI side is because it could artificially inhibit he service. For example, if we added this validation and later the service allows active-active mode with UltraPerformance SKU, then the CLI would be artificially constraining the behavior of the service.

   --public-ip-addresses [Required]: Specify a single public IP (name or ID) for an active-standby
                                      gateway. Specify two space-separated public IPs for an active-
                                      active gateway.
```
If you use the update command and change this, it will issue an info (visible with --verbose) that it is switching the gateway mode to active-standby or active-active accordingly.

def create_monitor_rule(name_of_rule, operator, metricName, threshold, resourceGroup, vm):
    
    odatatypeDS = "Microsoft.Azure.Management.Insights.Models.RuleMetricDataSource"
    resourceUri = "/subscriptions/your_subsription/resourceGroups/HCM-Monitoring-RG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/" + vm
    metricName = metricName
    odatatypeRC = "Microsoft.Azure.Management.Insights.Models.ThresholdRuleCondition"
    threshold = threshold
    operator = operator
    windowSize = '"PT5M"'
    timeAggregation = '"Average"'
    
    
    #az monitor alert-rules create -g 'your_resource_group' --alert-rule-resource-name 'rule2' --name 'rule2' --condition @travistested2 --is-enabled true --location westus
    json_string1 = "{ \"dataSource\": {" + '"odata.type": "' + odatatypeDS + '"' + ', "resourceUri" : ' + '"'+ resourceUri + '"' + ', "metricName" : ' + '"' + metricName + '",'  + "},"              
    json_string2 =  '"odata.type"' + ":" +  '"' + odatatypeRC + '"' +  "," + '"threshold"' + ":" + str(threshold)   + "," + '"operator"' + ":" + '"' + operator + '"' + "," + '"windowSize"' + ":" + windowSize + "," + '"timeAggregation"' + ":" + timeAggregation +  "}"
    condition = json_string1 + json_string2

#   print (condition)
 
    command = "az monitor alert-rules create --is-enabled true --location 'westus' -g " + "'" + resourceGroup + "'"  + \
     " --alert-rule-resource-name " + "'" + name_of_rule + "'" + \
    " --name " + "'" + name_of_rule + "'" + " --condition "  + "'" + condition + "'"
 
    print (command)

2- "Why support so many language specific SDKs and keep them essentially out of sync with one another and the PS cmdlets?"

That's essentially not true for Python, since CLI is in Python and CLI and Powershell are in sync. If unfortunately we were not able to keep the pace in a feature you need, just open an issue to describe the feature you see in Powershell that is not yet in Python and we will prioritize it. 
For other language, same applies, just open an issue if the SDK team of this language is not up-to-date with something you need.
PS is built top of a meta-description of the RestAPI, meaning that if PS has it, all other language can have it as well. It's just a matter of prioritizing it.

3- "fourth inconsistency", please open issue for any inconsistency so we can fix them. We're definitely not perfect, please point us to the actual issues you found so we can fix them and provide better experience,

Thank you,

I've taken a quick look at performance of conform. Top CPU usage: parsing CSV.

I dug deeper in to CSV parsing speed in Python and have three ideas for speeding it up. We could switch to Python 3 and dump unicodecsv, cutting CSV time to 50%. We could make our code more complicated by no longer using DictReader, cutting CSV time to 33%. Or we could switch to Pandas for CSV parsing, cutting CSV time to 25%. These are all significant optimizations, it'd make `nl` take maybe 90 minutes instead of 120, but they are not life-altering.

The other option is to rearchitect the code to do less CSV parsing. Right now each source is parsed twice: once from the source format to produce an "extracted" CSV file, and then the extracted CSV file is parsed again to produce the output. Eliminating that second step would also speed things up so it takes maybe 50-75% of the original time, but at the cost of making the code more complicated and harder to test.

I'm not enthusiastic about doing any of this work just now. Of all the options switching to Python3 is my favorite. Switching to DictReader is also probably not so hard.

More notes on my blog:
https://nelsonslog.wordpress.com/2015/02/25/openaddresses-optimization-some-baseline-timings/
https://nelsonslog.wordpress.com/2015/02/26/python-csv-benchmarks/

Another problem with implementing this at the moment is that the default SSL transport never calls `connection_lost()` so you'll get a hang. I might get around to submitting an issue to python for that at some point, but I've had to move on from asyncio entirely as a result of this and numerous other bugs, and the fact that there appears to be little clarity on how to correctly implement and use the interfaces.

As to why proper shutdown behaviour is required, it's because without it it is not safe for the client to close the event loop after the session is closed, and the client has no reliable way to wait for a time at which it _is_ safe to discard the loop.

SSL has a shutdown sequence that requires multiple steps, which in asyncio are naturally implemented asynchronously. Calling `close()` initiates that sequence, but the indication that it has finished is `connection_lost()`. If you don't wait for that sequence to complete before the aiohttp session's close has finished then the program will proceed without the SSL shutdown sequence having been completed. Worse, the program will proceed while the shutdown is _ongoing_, with tasks pending on the event loop. There is then _no way_ for the client to wait for those pending tasks to be complete before destroying the event loop, and destroying the event loop without them completing will cause "Task was destroyed but it is pending" warnings and a host of other problems. As @asvetlov  comments, even doing an `await sleep(0)` isn't sufficient in the client as there can be an arbitrary number of such awaits required before the queue is empty (2 in the particular case of SSL) and there is no way to explicitly check for the queue being empty.

The _only_ reason that these problems are not currently occurring is that asyncio's default SSL transport _isn't_ proceeding with its multi-step SSL shutdown sequence, so there are no tasks pending on the queue. This is a bug in that transport, and means that it never calls ```connection_lost()```.

If I'm understanding it correctly, this is a very nasty 2-way bug-bug dependency between aiohttp and asyncio. Fixing either of the two bugs will result in breakage: if you fix asyncio's SSL transport to do a correct SSL shutdown sequence without fixing aiohttp to wait for it to complete then existing clients who discard the event loop immediately after the aiohttp session is closed will be destroying the event loop with tasks pending; if you make aiohttp wait for the transport to do its shutdown without fixing the SSL transport to actually proceed with its shutdown, you will cause existing clients to hang.

It's a bit of a Gordian knot, if I'm understanding it correctly.

@ellisonbg there are a few ways to bring down the notebook. By far, the primary cause of that is flooding the frontend with iopub messages. Typically, on the frontend the limiting factor is putting output on the page. The most expensive version of which is usually stream output, which has to be merged and processed into HTML. This limits the number of those messages to avoid both clogging the network and choking the CPU in the browser by churning message handlers. The secondary fix, and probably the more important one, is setting an upper limit to how much stream output should be considered for merging when there are consecutive stream outputs. Technically, the two are independent changes, so I can separate them into two PRs, if you prefer. But both mitigate the problem of output flooding.

Note that from idle, it won't start buffering until the buffer is full, so you get 60 messages before any throttling begins. If you are sending messages at > 60 Hz *constantly*, something is probably wrong.

No problem; the package [pweave](http://mpastell.com/pweave/) is currently
my go to package for generating reports with python - though it's a long
way off R's [KnitR](http://yihui.name/knitr/) in terms of functionality;
have a look at the pweave examples [here](
http://mpastell.com/pweave/examples/) and KnitR examples [here](
https://github.com/yihui/knitr-examples).

The philosophy is admittedly slightly different from a notebook - your goal
from the start is to write code chronologically such that you 'Restart &
Run All' at the end to generate your document. However, enabling this
functionality could be quite simple: adding '[chunk](
http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/authoring_rcodechunks.html) [options](
http://mpastell.com/pweave/chunks.html)' to all code cells should allow
jupyter notebooks to be used for this purpose without any other changes.

From my perspective, the reason why this style of working is important is
twofold:

* Make creating reports easier
    - no faffing with latex (though you can use latex within the document
when you want - already implemented in jupyter markdown cells)
    - write the report as you code
    - allow inline references to the data and results e.g. instead of
writing 'The mean of the column was 2.6' you would write 'The mean of the
column was `np.mean(some_array)`', such that, if and when your data
changes, you simply regenerate your report, safe in the knowledge the text
is still correct
    - 1000s of researchers worldwide would benefit from the time saved
* Make reports reproducible
    - You know exactly what code you executed to create all the figures,
tables, and text
    - Reproducibility in academic research is currently pretty poor -
creating tools to make this easier will have widespread benefits to the
academic community

I think a good first step would be a markdown cell toolbar that controls
simple cell options like hiding (like the 'edit metadata' cell toolbar but
higher level). Most of the back end work appears to already be done - I
detail a solution [here](
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/31517194/how-to-hide-one-specific-cell-input-or-output-in-ipython-notebook/38040507#38040507)
that uses an nbextension - but an easy user interface that doesn't require
faffing with the cell metadata would be useful such that the user can see
at a glance what to expect the generated report to look like.

A more difficult next step, I imagine, would be thinking about how to
implement caching and whether that idea is compatible with the notebook
philosophy.

I'm keen to contribute if I can so do let me know if I can help in some way.

Also, it may be helpful here to note the similarity to how selection in emacs works. I will make this quick because I have to run to class in a minute...

* There is a cursor (overlaps a single character)
* There is a region (overlaps multiple characters and includes the cursor)
* All actions operate on the region
* You can tell the difference between the cursor and the region because they are usually different colors, and the cursor usually blinks

Is this going to be merged in? Just ran into this issue too. As @bintoro says, the current behavior is thoroughly baffling.

> I still prefer to have the logic and complexity of dealing with partial taken out and reduce the number of options accepted in Model constructor

I agree about minimizing bloat in the main API.

As for the actual functionality itself, consider this example:

```python
class User(Model):
    id = IntType()
    name = StringType()
    city = StringType()
    email = EmailType()
```

Let's say there's an existing row in a database:

id   | name      | city     | email
---- | --------- | -------- | ---------
99   | bintoro   | Helsinki | kalle@...

The client sends an update:

```json
PATCH /users/99 HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "city": null
}
```

Currently, updating the record using a Schematics instance created out of the payload would reset not only the city but **every** field. To reset just the city, an application would have to manually inspect the initial data.

Alternatively, loading the old data in an instance first and using `import_data` would reset **nothing**, since the incoming `None` is not considered any different from "not present".

I believe this is a very common use case, and therefore models should be able to deal with missing values natively. Introducing a parallel construct like `PartialModel` would add enormous complexity, as you'd then have to define the interactions between them and "regular" models.

> [...] as long as the non-partial use cases are unaffected, I am OK with the change.

Yes, this is the intent. Whether "no value" should be represented as `None` or `Undefined` is completely up to the application, and can be decided on a case-by-case basis.

Some defaults are probably going to change. For example, have you noticed that running `Model.validate()` will turn all `None`s into the respective fields' defaults? I find this completely bizarre and intend to reverse the behavior (i.e., `validate()` would set `apply_defaults=False`).

> One piece of advice, could we not expose `apply_defaults` and `init_values` as options/kwargs?

That's exactly where I'm going with this. If the existing `partial` parameter could work as a shortcut for the two main cases, then the two overrides (`apply_defaults` and `init_values`) wouldn't have to appear anywhere besides the main `import_loop` function.

The way I see it, the main loops in `transforms.py` should document all available switches, while the methods leading up to them could expose only the most relevant options. We'd have a `**kwargs` slot for the more esoteric arguments.

***

I'm still not sure what to think about having the `partial` parameter control the other two. It's not without its problems. In particular, there would be a disconnect as to the meaning of `partial` between `Model.__init__()` and `Model.validate()`.

A more compatible alternative would be to leave `partial` alone and add the `init_values` parameter to the `Model` constructor. Then, `init_values` could by default dictate the `apply_defaults` setting (unless overridden), since those two definitely go hand in hand.

That way,
  * the `required` check could continue to be performed upon import
  * `partial=True` could remain the default
  * the semantics of `partial` would be preserved exactly as is
  * creating a blank instance would only require switching `init_values` to False.

I set up a parallel branch that implements this and didn't run into any surprises. I think this is a better approach. Adding one more option to `Model.__init__` is not too bad. Those who don't need it can ignore it.

Those permission attributes are related to CAS, which is gone in .NET Core, so you shouldn't need them. .NET Core doesn't enforce security restrictions the way the old .NET Framework (sort of) did. Instead, if resources need to be protected, it's expected to be done at the OS level via ACLs or some similar mechanism.

So, it should be fine to just remove those attributes.

@toddrjen Would you be interested in writing a PR?  I don't need this feature personally (`edit` is more than enough for me) so I won't write it but I can provide some hints.

Note that this repo is somewhat unmaintained these days -- we're basically stuck with the difficulties in providing live output on Windows (#59).  If you only need Linux/MacOS support, and can use 2016b, then I'd suggest starting from my rewrite (https://github.com/anntzer/matlab_kernel), which does not depend on metakernel and provides a much nicer completion system.

Basically, you need to check entries that start with `function ...`, and modify `is_complete` (for such entries) to return true only if 1) the input parses correctly and 2) the input doesn't parse correctly if `end` is appended to it -- this will make sure that the function definition is correctly already terminated by an `"end"`.

Then such inputs need to be written to whatever directory with the correct filename.  For simplicity I'd suggest hardcoding it to `/tmp/matlab-kernel` (replace `/tmp` as appropriate for other OSes, see `tempfile`) and make it changeable via an environment variable (including to a relative path).

I forgot to mention one convention used in the loom-viewer:

For both row- and column- attributes, a "fake attribute" called `(original order)` is introduced in the viewer, which is just a set of ascending indices from 1 to row- or column-length. The viewer is very flexible in how it displays sparklinesIt is used to allow sorting data back to the original order it came in.

![sorting](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/259840/33129052-fffada74-cf8e-11e7-8eef-b778b53caa30.gif)

Let's call this a pseudo-attribute: it is metadata about the rows and colums, but not actually part of the loom-file itself.

The choice of using `( ... )` in the label stems from a time when only alphanumeric characters and underscores were allowed for attribute names. For a while we used to have another one too, I forgot the name.

We could put `(original order)` on a list of reserved attribute names, but perhaps it could also be useful to make it a convention to avoid keys wrapped in parenthesis in general. Other people start to write tools for `loompy`, they might have similar needs for pseudo-attributes, and this is a simple rule to explain. It also doesn't seem likely to interfere with other symbols that platforms might use to mark automatically generated data (an `_` or `#` prefix, that kind of thing)

I think it would be great to all standardize on Loom, and I just met with Rahul Satija yesterday who is working on full Loom support in Seurat. 

As for integration with scanpy it looks like it will be very easy. AnnData is semantically very close to Loom. Your [example](https://scanpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/scanpy.api.write.html) of creating an AnnData object translates directly:

```python
data=np.array([[1, 0], [3, 0], [5, 6]])
smp= {
    'row_names': ['name1', 'name2', 'name3'],
    'sanno1': ['cat1', 'cat2', 'cat2'],
    'sanno2': [2.1, 2.2, 2.3]
}
var= {
    'vanno1': [3.1, 3.2]
}
uns= {
    'sanno1_colors': "something",
    'uns2': "something else"
}
adata = AnnData(data, smp, var, uns)
ds = loompy.create(data, smp, var, file_attrs=uns)
```

(We only support "unstructured" data in the form of strings. For cross-language compatibility, I suggest the best solution is to JSON-encode the data as needed.)

Of course, that code creates an AnnData object in memory, but a Loom file on disk. There's a difference in philosophy: Loom was designed to support out-of-memory operation, while scanpy assumes the entire dataset is loaded in RAM. But this is also an opportunity: you could consider over time adding options to perform scanpy operations out-of-RAM to support very large datasets. And vice versa, for small datasets we also often just load the whole thing in memory.

Now, simplest would be for you to support reading and writing to Loom. But **much** better would be if you could support Loom as input to each of your functions, and write the result back to the file. That way if you do a PCA, say, you would only need to write the PCA coordinates as two attributes, instead of saving the whole updated AnnData object. E.g. it would be fantastic if we could do 

```python
with loompy.connect("filename.loom") as ds:
    scanpy.api.pp.filter_cells(ds)
```

and this would write a new cell attribute `cell_subset` to the file. 

This kind of support in scanpy, Seurat, possibly pagoda, and our own in-house analysis library (cytograph, not released yet) will allow complete mix and match of algorithms (I'm making up the rpy code but you get the idea):

```python
seurat = robjects.r['seurat']
pagoda = robjects.r['pagoda']
with loompy.connect("filename.loom") as ds:
    scanpy.api.pp.filter_cells(ds)
    cytograph.multiscale_knn(ds)
    seurat.cluster(ds)
    pagoda.diff_expression(ds)
```

(to avoid reading the whole matrix in memory at each call, Loom should support memoization; I will open a separate issue about this)

As for conventions, we use cells in columns and genes in rows. This is usually the opposite of what scipy expects (unless you're clustering the genes, for example). In order to maximize interoperability, we also need to agree on attribute names, both for metadata and for intermediate results (e.g. the result of a PCA, or filtering, etc). I'll open a separate issue for this too.

@jsenecal I'm not sure who's complaining about the package? Several of our developers have made contributions to this package over the last six months, I'm very glad it's available. In fact, our product depends on it. 

That's why I'm pretty worried that a critical issue like this was commented on with "I don't see anything directly actionable with this issue." ... perhaps my comment was a bit hyperbolic, but if I re-state it as "completely unsuitable for any production environment **with** large data sets" you might understand where I'm coming from.

Here's a scenario:

If you have a single model, e.g. Book, which has four relations e.g. Author, Publisher, CopyrightHolder, Category.

To display 25 books in DRF without any includes, I would need a single query: SELECT * FROM book.

To display 25 books DRF-JSONAPI without any includes, I would need either:
a) 1 query ala SELECT * FROM books LEFT JOIN author LEFT JOIN publisher LEFT JOIN CopyrightHolder LEFT JOIN Category
b) 4 queries with prefetches.

Let's say I have 1M books, 50k authors, 10k categories, 10k copyrightholders. In the select_related scenario, you've just created a in-memory table with 1e18 rows ... to display 25 rows, with no included relationships. So select_related is only going to work if you have a small dataset or a small volume of traffic.

So maybe we can do the prefetch option. Sure now we need 4x more queries, but at least they don't cause the database to melt down.

However, this doesn't scale with the app complexity. If you want to include the Author, Category and CopyrightHolder with the books, then you have to prefetch any relationships those models might have just to include them! You start ending up writing your ViewSet's like this:

```
class BookViewSet(ModelViewSet):
    queryset = Book.objects.all().prefetch_related(
        'book__author__tags',
        'book__author__client__tags',
        'book__author__client__primary_contact',
        'book__author__client__billingcard',
        'book__author__client__account',
        'book__copyrightholder__client__account_manager',
        'book__copyrightholder__client__clientgroup__accounts',
        'book__copyrightholder__branch__groups',
        'book__copyrightholder__branch__user_set',
        'book__publisher__address',
        'book__publisher__servicecontract_set',
        'book__publisher__account_manager',
        'book__property__billingcard',
        'book__service_group',
        'book__type__standard',
        'book__category_set__type',
        'book__type__categorytype_set',
    )
```

This is not a contrived example. That's an actual ModelViewSet we have though I had to slightly obfuscate the model names.

Once you have even a moderately complex app, it becomes impossible to stop query explosions as new relationships get added -- every model that might include that model's API needs to be updated.

> a string of 5 NULL characters is just the empty string

There is a difference between how Z3 represents strings and how C represents strings.
A C string is a sequence of characters **up until the first null termination**.
While a Z3 size 5 string is a sequence of 5 characters.
What you're looking for is indexof:

    (declare-const a String)
    
    (assert (= (str.indexof a "\x00" 0) 5))
    
    (check-sat)
    (get-value (a))

Which gives:

    sat
    ((a "�\x04\x02\x01\x01\x00"))

If you don't like \x00 as default value you can try to specify:

    smt.string_solver=z3str3

Which outputs visible ascii as its default (from what I've seen at least):

    sat
    ((a "aaaa3\x00\x00e"))

It looks like this function uses `theano.tensor.extra_ops.to_one_hot` to convert the targets to a one-hot representation if necessary. I believe this may be the culprit. Are you passing the targets in the form of a vector of integers? (If not then it must be something else.)

This `to_one_hot` function ( https://github.com/Theano/Theano/blob/ecd9dc0ba6fa70b7f65c6de620c21e502c414c5e/theano/tensor/extra_ops.py#L1063-L1088 ) uses `T.set_subtensor`, which is probably what generates the `Nonzero` and `AdvancedIncSubtensor` stuff.

In Lasagne, we have an alternative implementation which uses a trick to avoid this ( https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/0d1015e28d957e0573dfca97c82e7cbe2ab36b32/lasagne/utils.py#L102-L136 ). It indexes an identity matrix instead. I'm not 100% sure if this is always moved to the GPU either, but it's worth a shot. It also only works for a small number of labels. If you have K=1000s of labels this will pointlessly allocate a large K=K identity matrix, which you would probably want to avoid.

A simple workaround is of course to just pass in the labels in one-hot format yourself.

> This PR has 110 commits, some of which only change a single word, or fix things in an example that was subsequently removed. In other PRs we usually take care to squash certain commits to not fill up Lasagne's history with things not worth preserving (such as spelling or pep8 mistakes being introduced and removed), and maybe also to not skew the commit-count-based contributor statistics. In any case, this is a long-running PR, it definitely has some history worth preserving, and cleaning up the history by cherry-picking and squashing commits would be very cumbersome (compared to our usual PRs of 3 or 4 commits by a single author). So I'd be fine with making an exception.

I can do some squashing of trivial commits, but I'd also like to preserve the commit history as much as possible - a major difference here is that I and many others have been using this codebase for many months, and it (like Lasagne) has changed a good deal in that time, so it's very nice for me to be able to retrieve the codebase at a certain point in time for backwards/parallel-compatibility.  Squashing functional-change commits would make this impossible.  But, you're agreeing with me here, so let's leave it at that :)

> When we were getting started with the angr project, the big question that got tossed around a lot is "do we model the environment at the library level, or do we model it at the system level?" basically, do we implement simprocedures for every library function, or do we implement every syscall and make sure our loader is perfect?"

I was, and still am a big advocate for the former. Implementing `SimProcedures` for libraries can be done _on demand_, is usually easily debuggable, and are way more scalable than implementing syscalls (you can introduce different limits in individual library functions, or `SimProcedures`). That said, we do spend a lot of effort (mostly by @rhelmot) in correctly loading binaries and emulating syscalls.

Doing research on the CGC platform is a great plus - you don't have to worry about libraries unless you really want and have to. However, angr is also targeting both academic research and real-world binary analysis, so we do care about problems in binary loading, syscall/SimProcedure implementations, etc.

There is a script in the repository `angr-dev` called `checkout_at.py` if I recall correctly that will check out all the repositories from a specific date. You want the date of the CGC last year, August 4 2016.

We can't devote time to making sure that the full mechaphish system integration still works, but most of the components (with the exception of rex, which has been picked recently up by a third party with the intent of upgrading it to the newest angr release) should still work.

If you have a vehicle flying a mission and you send messages over its telemetry link you can do pretty much whatever you like since there is no way in MAVLink 1.0 to know that the messages are from a trusted source. So you could send messages to  change the RC values if you liked or you could delete the mission or change it. The autopilot is your slave - even if you have set it to obey a certain GCS by its system id, that ID can be set in your messages. 

-  I'm not sure how much power the transmitter has to lock you out by changing the mode to manual.
- If the original telemetry system is still in range it can send messages too - you can't stop it, though I guess you might be able to change the parameters so that it won't listen to the original GCS.
- You can't just modify one motor power because the autopilot doesn't expose single motors. You can change vehicle parameters so that the autopilot is misinterpreting what inputs are coming from where.

Anyway, again, given that there is no security for the messages to/from the vehicle, simulating an attack is just "sending commands". I guess you could also simulate the operator reacting, or as @mikerob suggests, see what happens if you send commands when the vehicle is under autonomous control.

  2. Driver installation issues (In 99% of cases this means Ubuntu/Linux)

Hi @yoojinoh! DummyVecEnv and SubprocVecEnv are doing the same job (in fact, in terms of functionality, one can be replaced with the other - they *should* have the same API defined by abstract superclass VecEnv), the only difference is that SubprocVecEnv runs steps in multiple environments in subprocesses, i.e. actually in parallel, whereas DummyVecEnv runs steps for multiple environments one after another and is not using any kind of parallelism. 
To elaborate on that point, consider a call to venv.step(), where venv is in instance of either DummyVecEnv or SubprocVecEnv. DummyVecEnv interates over multiple environments in a for loop, calling env.step() for the wrapped environments one after another. If using Nenv environments, and each of them takes time Tstep to make a step, DummyVecEnv.step will take Nenv * Tstep.   SubprocVecEnv first sends the data to all of the subprocesses, and then waits for all of them to send the data back - this way, computation of environment steps happens in parallel. This way, SubprocVecEnv only takes time max(Tstep_i) to run a step.  Natually, when using only one environment, SubprocVecEnv does not give any gain in speed (in fact, it introduces some inter-process communication overhead), that's why one num_envs == 1, we use DummyVecEnv.

Circling back to answer my own question, it is an intentional limitation of the graphQL spec that means Input and Output types are different. Quoting the spec: 
```
Fields can define arguments that the client passes up with the query, to configure their behavior. These inputs can be Strings or Enums, but they sometimes need to be more complex than this.

The Object type defined above is inappropriate for re‐use here, because Objects can contain fields that express circular references or references to interfaces and unions, neither of which is appropriate for use as an input argument. For this reason, input objects have a separate type in the system.

An Input Object defines a set of input fields; the input fields are either scalars, enums, or other input objects. This allows arguments to accept arbitrarily complex structs.
```
