There is a sutle and intresting reason behind the increase in RAM. Here is what I figured out:

Empty classes use 1 byte size to ensure they always get a unique address. "Empty base optimization" makes an exception for when an empty class is inherited and is used as base class. Our `InstanceLocator` is an empty base class (in the single instance case), so it's optimized and does not add any data.

On the other hand, C++ standard does not allow two objects of same type to share the same memory address. So if we have a class that inherits from `InstanceLocator` and then the first member variable defined in the class happens to be a type that also inherits from `InstanceLocator`, to ensure the two `InstanceLocator` objects do not share the same memory, some extra padding bytes will be added by the complier (please see [empty base optimization](http://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/language/ebo)).

`Ip6:Mpl` is an example for this case: It is itself an `InstanceLocator` and the first data member variable is a `TimerMilli mSeedSetTimer` which again inherits from `InstanceLocator`.

We can avoid this by re-arranging the member variable declarations (when possible).
Alternatively, we can change `TimerMilli/Usec` or `Tasklet` (which are the more commonly used member variable types) to inherit from a different locator class type

My general process for dealing with multiple versions of tools on Linux is simply to install them into different paths.  For example, I have:

```
/opt:
    gcc-arm-none-eabi-4_7-2013q3
    gcc-arm-none-eabi-4_8-2014q3
    gcc-arm-none-eabi-4_9-2015q3
    gcc-arm-none-eabi-5_4-2016q2
    gcc-arm-none-eabi-5_4-2016q3
```

Then I simply configure my `$PATH` to point to the proper directory when compiling.

Alternatively, if you look in each of the example convenience Makefiles (e.g. [examples/Makefile-cc2538](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/Makefile-cc2538)), you'll see the following where you can specify all the tools:

```
AR                              = arm-none-eabi-ar

CC                              = arm-none-eabi-gcc
CXX                             = arm-none-eabi-g++
LD                              = arm-none-eabi-ld
STRIP                           = arm-none-eabi-strip
NM                              = arm-none-eabi-nm
RANLIB                          = arm-none-eabi-ranlib
OBJCOPY                         = arm-none-eabi-objcopy
```

Feel free to change those to whatever executable names/paths you like.

The APIs should be consistent.
1. OpenThread Client API: `otGetExtendedAddress` and `otSetExtendedAddress` take/provide the EUI-64 where the OUI is the first bytes.
2. OpenThread Platform API: `otPlatRadioSetExtendedAddress` takes the EUI-64 where the OUI is the last bytes.  Currently, `otPlatRadioGetExtendedAddress` does not exist.  The radios I have worked with all expect the OUI in the last bytes and is consistent with the IEEE 802.15.4 header encoding, which is why the Platform API uses this order.

I double checked that even if the versioneer module `versioneer` is installed on the user's machine, `versioneer.py` is required to be present in the same folder as `setup.py`.  The module is mainly just an installer for `versioneer.py`.

>> What does versioneer.py do and what does _version.py do? I'm a little confused...

I think the design is a little confusing, but this small section of the readme explains the different purposes of the two:
https://github.com/warner/python-versioneer#theory-of-operation

Basically during the initial install of versioneer the `do_setup` method from `versioneer.py` generated the `_version.py` file based on values in `site.cfg`, added it to MANIFEST.in, etc.  The methods related to use in `setup.py` are in `versioneer.py` while the `_version.py` has the functions for querying versions from `git`.

For some types of builds (e.g. source distribution) `versioneer.py` replaces `_version.py` with a more minimal file as the version is fixed for that particular source release.  I tried it and the version that ends up in a source distribution looks something like:
```python
# This file was generated by 'versioneer.py' (0.18) from
# revision-control system data, or from the parent directory name of an
# unpacked source archive. Distribution tarballs contain a pre-generated copy
# of this file.

import json

version_json = '''
{
 "date": "2017-05-10T18:19:06-0400",
 "dirty": false,
 "error": null,
 "full-revisionid": "e276406ca3954dc7e81bf030a2672fac0fc0ac43",
 "version": "v0.10.4+65.ge276406"
}
'''  # END VERSION_JSON


def get_versions():
    return json.loads(version_json)
```

I think we could address them in a couple of ways:

1. Adding `print_created(...)`/`print_destroyed()`/etc. that prints (in addition to tracking) so that the constructor output information is preserved in examples where it's useful to preserve it.  In some cases it isn't particularly useful as example output, but tracking that construction/destruction is happening as desired is a useful test.  For testing purposes we'd still get the tracking information, but for output purposes we could just ignore all the constructor/destructor output.

2. PR #321 ought to be able to get rid of all of the actual output of number of constructors in favour of just testing their values so that the bits of the .py printing the number of constructors (which really isn't useful as an example, but is just about testing) can go.

3. I think it ought to be possible to remove the `DEF_CSTATS_...` macros in favour of a single function exported from example.cpp that takes the class as an argument, so that the definitions can be removed from the example classes.

With those changes, the only things left in the examples would be constructors/destructors containing either a `print_...()` or `track_...()` depending on whether printing the constructor/destructors are useful, and then the .py code to test that the number of constructions/destructions/etc. is acceptable.

Certbot does restarts on it's own after adding the challenge configuration for Apache.

I'll take a wild guess what's going on in here; your server is under heavy or moderate load, serving requests that take a long time to serve. What Certbot uses for Apache restarts isn't the actual "restart" verb for apachectl, but "graceful" which restarts the workers but only after they have finished serving the requests, in order to not to kill active connections. Now the latency is too long or available connection pool is exhausted because of the load, and because the active connections haven't been forcefully shut down so Apache isn't able to serve the challenge. 

What you could do to debug the problem: after Apache has been serving for a while, try simple curl requests directly to the IP, with whatever Host header not in use, this is pretty much the thing that Let's Encrypt is doing in the renewal. for example `curl -v -k https://your.ip.address/ -H "Host: somethingsomething.whatever.invalid"`. And check how long it takes for your server to answer, perhaps by prepending the command with `time`.

> Are you proposing we remove the notion of a potentially pluggable (#790) display interface from Certbot and just use a 3rd party library or just deprecate what we currently call IDisplay in favor of a new interface?

My intent was to just propose deprecating the current interface and replacing it with a new one (which might need a different name). I think the advantage of that approach is that it allows the new interface to be whatever it "should" be, unburdened with backwards compatibility, and `IDisplay` can continue to exist as a shim around the new interface to provide compatibility with existing code for whatever period of time is necessary.

However, your question got me thinking about this more critically. I think trying to design the display code so that it could be pluggable in the future has added a significant amount of complexity, and I'm not sure that what we have today is remotely close to being plugable. So much of the code that uses `IDisplay` is tightly coupled to the current implementation (e.g., assuming that validation happens out of band, after the user inputs a value, really doesn't make sense for a GUI) that introducing a new display implementation would basically require an audit of all code that uses the interface.

I think it's good to keep the future in mind when designing things like this, but I don't see pluggable display as necessary for anything in the next three months/year. A clear, concise, cohesive display API might simplify complexity and maintenance costs for now, to allow you to focus on your current goals (and wouldn't put you in a worse position than today for whatever the future holds).

And, while I'm pondering, I wonder if a completely different approach, like strict separation of "display code" from "business logic" might be better in the long term, both for enabling different display methods (providing a good user experience in a GUI is going to require more than a new implementation of `IDisplay`) and for allowing third parties to build on Certbot (as it is _much_ easier to provide a stable API for the "business logic" if it is decoupled from interactivity).

Thanks @kaefert. But there will be problem in this approach.

If letsencrypt is going to just use publicsuffix from [golang/net](https://github.com/golang/net), there will be problem. According to @bradfitz in [golang/net#15518](https://github.com/golang/go/issues/15518#issuecomment-217312171), they have no intention to keep re-generating their publicsuffix frequently. That means if letsencrypt is using the exact version of publicsuffix in [golang/net](https://github.com/golang/net), it will almost never be updated.

The sensible solution would be either:
1. To generate the [table.go](https://github.com/letsencrypt/net/blob/master/publicsuffix/table.go) locally instead of using the upstream generated list.
2. To use the latest publicsuffix dynamically instead of hard-code to the source.

> So, the main use case for this is that someone had already put a certbot command in their crontab, but then the packaging system goes and puts it in there again.

That's definitely the biggest use case. Another one is a user tries to manually run Certbot at the same time as the cronjob happens to fire. Even less likely is two users on the same system try to run Certbot at the same time using some of the same files.

> If we quit, we definitely won't deadlock, but we could easily always fail to run either of them, or at least one of them.

> We probably want both to complete successfully, no? Which means we do want to block on the lock.

Hm. While this might be a nice property, I personally think achieving it has other consequences that outweigh the benefits. Having a single instance or even all concurrently running Certbot instances fail isn't a big deal in my opinion. We tell people to run the cronjob twice a day and this advice is followed by our package maintainers so I think a single failure due to lock contention hardly matters at all. In fact, if people have e-mail from cron set up properly this will help notify them that there is a problem and there are two cron jobs trying to run Certbot on their system.

If we block and have more than one lock (including any additional locks in the plugins), we have ensure the locking order never changes or risk deadlock. While we could definitely do this, I'm not sure it's worth the extra complexity and risk of bugs. If we have a single lock file, the risk of deadlock goes away but we now either break people who are running Certbot without root, have an insecure lockfile, or put a lockfile in a single core directory allowing users to easily shoot themselves in the foot without knowing it.

> And you're right, the no-sudo is a hard problem. Which is why I liked having a particular file in /tmp. Because while other users can maliciously change things, we're not doing this for security, we're doing it to avoid unintentionally getting to an invalid state.

You're right that the purpose of this PR is to prevent multiple instances of Certbot from putting any of the files it modifies into an invalid state. Putting a lockfile in `/tmp` though allows arbitrary users to circumvent or abuse these protections. They can acquire the lock themselves and prevent Certbot from running and renewing your certs. They can delete the lockfile after it's created removing these protections entirely. While maybe neither of these things are a big deal, it opens Certbot up to other problems that we do not have with another approach.

Despite me standing my ground a bit here, I'm certainly open to changing things if we come up with a better solution. The only potential problem I am aware of with the current approach though is we don't block, but changing this comes with other potential problems I'd like to avoid.

`python-prompt-toolkit` ([docs](https://python-prompt-toolkit.readthedocs.io/), [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/prompt_toolkit)) seems to be relatively lightweight, very general purpose (designed to work on a variety of OSes and over telnet/SSH) and provide a superset of the functionality Certbot needs.

@jgillula 
## Postfix plugin
So there are two main things that this Installer does:
 1. Configures TLS Postfix parameters in `main.cf`. The parameters for this come [recommended](http://www.postfix.org/TLS_README.html) for a balance of security & compatibility.
 2. Write the per-domain TLS policies from `starttls-policy` into a [format that Postfix understands](http://www.postfix.org/postconf.5.html#smtp_tls_policy_maps).

The code for [1] is in [`deploy_cert`](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/5576/files#diff-62ce583f47d3952d57797e5bf0a01da2L159), which
  - Sets the key/cert for `smtpd`, the MTA server (mail receiver),
  - Sets reasonable TLS params for both the MTA server and client.
  - Sets the path to the policy file (from [2]) 

The code for [2] is in [`write_domainwise_tls_policies`](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/5576/files#diff-62ce583f47d3952d57797e5bf0a01da2R130), which loops through the domains
and [writes their policy](http://www.postfix.org/postconf.5.html#smtp_tls_policy_maps) to the appropriate file.

## Other stuff
The other large portion of diffs in this pull request pertain to refactoring a lot of the code around running command-line utilities like `postfix` and `postconf`. Brad started this refactor in `postconf.py` and I finished it up (Look at all this [deleted code!](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/pull/5576/files#diff-62ce583f47d3952d57797e5bf0a01da2L228)), and also added tests in `postconf_test.py`. There's also wrappers around the `postfix` command-line util in `util.py`. As a result, I also moved a lot of the tests from `installer_test.py` into `postconf_test.py` (since the code for running configuration edits was no longer in `installer.py`.

## Things that are left
There are always more tests to write, and I have to write an integration test for the per-domain policy things, so I can catch regressions easily. The most pressing thing I'm working on right now is standardizing and thoroughly documenting the API for fetching policies in the `starttls-policy` package.

`/home/bassler/foobar/funk.dat`
- `funk.dat` : filename
- `funk` : basename
- `/home/bassler/foobar` : absolute directory `abs_dir`
- `./foobar` :  relative directory `rel_dir`
- `/home/bassler/foobar/funk.dat` : absolute `abs_path`
- `foobar/funk.dat` : (relative) path `rel_path`
- `/home/bassler/foobar/funk` : root path `root_path`
- `./foobar/funk` : root path `root_path`

Prefixes:
 - 'abs' -> absolute path to file or directory, starting with `/` or `C:\` (see `os.path.abspath`)
 - 'root' -> root part of path (may be absolute or relative, see `os.path.splitext`)
 - 'rel' -> relative path (see `os.path.relpath`)

Today I've executed the driver on an actual Lakeshore Model 372 instrument. Outcome:
* the driver seems to work
* `wait_until_set_point_reached` method has a bug in `log.debug` (left a separate comment about it)
* scientists requested the ability to turn the channels on and off. This is available via `INSET` command (but it also has multiple inputs...). The rationale for turning the channels off is the following: when Lakeshore measures values at sensors, it goes through the enabled ones one by one, sets proper ranges in its internal lock-in, etc. The more channels you have enabled, the more time it takes between measurements of values for a given sensor. Since the heater uses only one sensor, the other sensors can be turned off to increase the effective time of the heater's feedback loop "response". A minor detail here is that this command also allows to change the "curve" (temperature-resistance) of the sensor - users either should not be able to change it, or there should be huge warnings and banners and prompts before changing it, because you do not want to suddenly change the curve for which the sensor was calibrated for.

The main difference between our object store and a parameter server is that objects in the object store are "immutable" once they have been written. There are probably some performance gains that could be obtained for certain applications (e.g., A3C) by mutating and reusing the same shared-memory buffers (however, I'm not sure exactly how large these gains are). When you're doing dense updates anyway, the extra copy is often not a big deal. However, we'd need to figure out the best way to expose mutable objects through the API (e.g., what are the semantics on multiple machines, do we want to do anything to prevent the data from being corrupted by non-atomic writes, etc).

As for running out of memory, that could certainly happen if you collect more arrays together on one driver than you have room for. It's possible that we could get around this by implementing an efficient all reduce or something like that.

There are a few cases to consider:

with up-front check:
 - If channel is not hosting, 3 API calls (get hosted, get stream)
 - If channel is hosting, 3 API calls (get hosted, get stream)
 - If channel is hosting and `disable-hosting`, 1 API call (get hosted)

without up-front check:
 - If channel is not hosting. 2 API calls (get stream)
 - If channel is hosting, 5 API calls (get stream, get hosted, get stream)
 - If channel is hosting and `disable-hosting`, 2 API call (get stream)


With out the up-front check, the "normal" case of a channel not hosting is one API call faster - this might be an improvement if this is the most common use case. Any case where the channel is hosting, there will be more API calls.

This is a bug in 0.18.0: https://github.com/google/yapf/issues/458

After the bug fix, yapf will reformat all our `async def`s with long argument lists even *more* aggressively, because it will switch to using an 8 space indent like it already does for regular `def`s. I.e., with 0.17.0 it does:

```python
def foo(
        a,
        b,
        c,
):
    pass

async def foo(
    a,
    b,
    c,
):
    pass
```
and after https://github.com/google/yapf/issues/458 is fixed it will do:
```python
def foo(
        a,
        b,
        c,
):
    pass

async def foo(
        a,
        b,
        c,
):
    pass
```

Given that 0.18.0 is just broken, I'm going to pin 0.17.0 for now. Given that we're using the combination of `split_before_first_argument=True` and `dedent_closing_brackets=True`, then the old 4-space indent is arguably nicer – the 8-space indent is designed to avoid formatting like:
```python
def foo(
    a,
    b,
    c):
    pass
```
when using `split_before_first_argument=True` and `dedent_closing_brackets=False`. I'm not sure if that's something we can fix or not, though, and we can re-evaluate once the `async def` bug is fixed.

NOTES FROM PEP 257 https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/
The docstring for a module should generally list the classes, exceptions and 
functions (and any other objects) that are exported by the module, with a 
one-line summary of each. (These summaries generally give less detail than the 
summary line in the object's docstring.) The docstring for a package (i.e., 
the docstring of the package's __init__.py module) should also list the 
modules and subpackages exported by the package. 

The docstring for a class should summarize its behavior and list the public methods and instance variables. If the class is intended to be subclassed, and has an additional interface for subclasses, this interface should be listed separately (in the docstring). The class constructor should be documented in the docstring for its __init__ method. Individual methods should be documented by their own docstring. 

The docstring for a function or method should summarize its behavior and document its arguments, return value(s), side effects, exceptions raised, and restrictions on when it can be called (all if applicable). Optional arguments should be indicated. It should be documented whether keyword arguments are part of the interface.
